Ayahs

ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَهُ ٱلْحَمْدُ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ (١)
All praise belongs to Allah to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth. To Him belongs all praise in the Hereafter, and He is the All-wise, the All-aware. (1)
يَعْلَمُ مَا يَلِجُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا يَخْرُجُ مِنْهَا وَمَا يَنزِلُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَمَا يَعْرُجُ فِيهَا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلرَّحِيمُ ٱلْغَفُورُ (٢)
He knows whatever enters into the earth and whatever emerges from it, and whatever descends from the sky and whatever ascends into it, and He is the All-merciful, the All-forgiving. (2)

Commentary

He is the Owner of All Things and the Knower of All Things

Five surahs of the Holy Quran begin with the praise of the Lord. In three of these surahs, the praise and glorification of Allah are for the creation of the heavens, the earth, and other beings (Surah Saba, Surah Fatir, and Surah Al-An'am).

In one surah (Surah Al-Kahf), this praise and glorification are for the revelation of the Quran upon the pure heart of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله.

Meanwhile, in Surah Al-Hamd, a comprehensive expression is used that encompasses all these matters: "الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ" (Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds).

In any case, the discussion at the beginning of Surah Saba concerns the praise and thanks of Allah due to His ownership and sovereignty in this world and the Hereafter. He says:

"Praise belongs to Allah, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" (الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ).

"And to Him belongs praise in the Hereafter" (وَ لَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ).

In this way, the sovereignty and ownership of both worlds belong to Him. Every blessing, every gift, every benefit and grace, and every balanced, marvelous, and wondrous creation—all belong to His Pure Essence. For this reason, "Hamd" (Praise), which in reality is praise for "voluntary good acts," all returns to Him. Even if some creatures are worthy of praise and glorification, they too are a reflection of His existence and a ray of His actions and attributes.

Therefore, whoever praises or glorifies anything in this world, this praise and glorification ultimately return to His Pure Essence. As the poet says: I am happy with the world because the world is happy through Him; I am in love with the whole world, for the whole world is from Him.

And at the end of the verse, He adds: "And He is the All-Wise, the All-Aware" (وَ هُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْخَبِيرُ).

It is based on His profound wisdom that this wondrous system governs the world, and it is based on His knowledge and awareness that everything is placed in its proper position, and every being possesses what it needs.

Regarding what is meant by the praise and glorification of Allah in the Hereafter, commentators have discussed much.

Some have said that although the Hereafter is not a place of religious obligation (taklif), the servants of Allah will praise and glorify Him there out of love, and they will find pleasure in His praise and glorification! Others have said: the inhabitants of Paradise praise Him for His grace, and the inhabitants of Hell for His justice! Sometimes it is said: humans in this world often lack pure praise due to various veils over their hearts and minds; however, in the Resurrection, when all veils are removed and—according to the verse "الْمُلْكُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلَّهِ" (Sovereignty on that Day belongs to Allah)—Allah's ownership over the expanse of existence becomes manifest to everyone, they will all open their tongues in His praise and glorification with complete sincerity of intention.

Furthermore, in this world, humans might become negligent and perceive beings as independent of Allah's Essence and praise them; but there, where the connection of all things to His Pure Essence becomes as clear as the connection of a sunbeam to the solar disk, no one will praise anyone but Him.

Beyond all this, it is repeatedly mentioned in the Holy Quran that the inhabitants of Paradise praise Allah there: "وَ آخِرُ دَعْواهُمْ أَنِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ":

"And the end of their cry will be: 'Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds'" (Yunus, Verse 10).

Elsewhere we read: when the believers enter the gardens of eternal Paradise, they say: "Praise be to Allah, who has removed from us [all] sorrow" (وَ قالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي أَذْهَبَ عَنَّا الْحَزَنَ) (Fatir, 34).

This praise is not only from the tongues of humans and angels; rather, the whisper of His praise and glorification reaches the perceptive ear from every atom of the universe. There is no being except that it celebrates His praise and glorification.

The next verse proceeds to describe a portion of the Lord's infinite knowledge, in accordance with the description of Allah as "All-Wise" and "All-Aware" in the previous verse, saying: "He knows what penetrates into the earth and what emerges from it" (يَعْلَمُ ما يَلِجُ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ ما يَخْرُجُ مِنْها).

Yes, He is aware of all the raindrops and flood waves that sink into the depths of the earth, reaching the impermeable layer where they concentrate and are stored for humans.

He is aware of the seeds of plants that are scattered across the expanse of the earth with the help of the wind or insects, sinking into the ground and one day turning into a lush tree or a vibrant plant.

He knows of the roots of trees as they advance into the depths of the earth in search of water and nutrients.

He knows of the waves of electricity, the various gases, and the particles of air that penetrate into the earth; of the living creatures that go into the ground and give it life; and also of the treasures, buried hoards, and the bodies of the dead—whether human or non-human—that are buried in this vast earth. Yes, He is aware of all of these.

Likewise, He knows of the plants that emerge from the earth, of the humans who have risen from it, of the springs that gush forth from it, of the gases that rise from it, of the volcanoes that flare up from it, and of the insects that have nests within the earth and emerge from it. In short, He is informed and aware of all beings that pour out from the depths of the earth, whether they are things we know or do not know.

Then He adds: "And what descends from the heaven and what ascends into it" (وَ ما يَنْزِلُ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَ ما يَعْرُجُ فِيها).

He is informed of the raindrops, the life-giving rays of the sun, the powerful waves of revelation and heavenly laws, the angels who descend to earth to deliver a message or perform other missions, the cosmic rays that descend to earth from beyond the atmosphere, and the meteors and wandering pebbles of space that are attracted toward the earth.

And also of the deeds of the servants that ascend to heaven, of the angels who return to heaven after delivering their message, of the devils who go to the heavens to eavesdrop, of the branches of tall trees that reach toward the sky, of the vapors that rise from the sea and form clouds in the sky, and of the sigh that rises from the heart of an oppressed person and ascends to heaven—yes, He is aware of all of these.

Does anyone besides Him have knowledge of these matters? Does the knowledge of all human scientists encompass even a corner of this information?! And at the end, He adds: "And He is the Most Merciful, the All-Forgiving" (وَ هُوَ الرَّحِيمُ الْغَفُورُ).

The description of Allah with these two attributes here is either because among the things that ascend to heaven are the deeds of the servants and their souls, and it is He who places them under the cover of His mercy and forgiveness.

Or it is because the descent of heavenly blessings and gifts is a product of His mercy, and the righteous deeds that ascend from the servants—according to the principle "وَ الْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ" (and the righteous deed He uplifts)—are subject to His forgiveness.

Or it is that those who give thanks for these blessings are subject to mercy, and those who fall short, if they do not exceed the limits, are subject to forgiveness.

In short, the above verse has a vast and expansive meaning in all its dimensions, and it should not be limited to a single direction.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَا تَأْتِينَا ٱلسَّاعَةُ ۖ قُلْ بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّى لَتَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ ۖ لَا يَعْزُبُ عَنْهُ مِثْقَالُ ذَرَّةٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَآ أَصْغَرُ مِن ذَٰلِكَ وَلَآ أَكْبَرُ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ (٣)
The faithless say, ‘The Hour will not overtake us.’ Say, ‘Yes, indeed it will surely overtake you, by my Lord,’ the Knower of the Unseen; not [even] an atom’s weight escapes Him in the heavens or in the earth, nor [is there] anything smaller than that nor bigger, but it is in a manifest Book, (3)
لِّيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ (٤)
that He may reward those who have faith and do righteous deeds.’ For such there will be forgiveness and a noble provision. (4)
وَٱلَّذِينَ سَعَوْ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا مُعَـٰجِزِينَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مِّن رِّجْزٍ أَلِيمٌ (٥)
But those who contend with Our signs seeking to frustrate [their purpose], for such is a painful punishment due to defilement. (5)

Commentary

By the Lord, the Resurrection Shall Come!

The preceding verses, while speaking of Monotheism and the attributes of Allah, were laying the groundwork for the issue of the Resurrection; for, as we shall see, the difficulties of the discussion on the Resurrection cannot be resolved except through the infinite knowledge of the Truth (Allah).

Therefore, in the verses under discussion, it first states: "The disbelievers said: 'It is a lie that a Resurrection lies ahead of us; the Hour will never come to us'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لا تَأْتِينَا السَّاعَةُ).

Not only for us, but for none of humanity is there a Resurrection! They wanted to gain freedom of action through this claim and perform whatever deed they were capable of, in the hope that there is no account, no book, and no justice.

However, since the proofs of the Resurrection are clear, the Quran, with a decisive sentence and in the form of stating a conclusion, tells the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Yes, by my Lord, the Resurrection shall surely come to all of you" (قُلْ بَلى‌ وَ رَبِّي لَتَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ‌).

The emphasis on the word "Rabb" (Lord) is because the Resurrection is among the affairs of Lordship (rububiyyah). How is it possible for Allah to be the Owner and Nurturer of human beings and lead them on the path of perfection, yet leave them halfway, such that everything ends with death, and their life becomes aimless and their creation null and void?! In Surah At-Taghabun, verse 7, this same attribute is emphasized, where He says:

زَعَمَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنْ لَنْ يُبْعَثُوا قُلْ بَلى‌ وَ رَبِّي لَتُبْعَثُنَّ ثُمَ‌ لَتُنَبَّؤُنَّ بِما عَمِلْتُمْ:

"The disbelievers claimed that they would never be resurrected. Say: 'Yes, by my Lord, you will all surely be resurrected in the Resurrection, then you will all be informed of your deeds and their consequences!'" And since one of the objections of the opponents of the Resurrection was that when the human body becomes dust and its parts are scattered across the earth, who can recognize and gather them and return them to a new life? On the other hand, who can keep the account of all these deeds of the servants, in secret and in public, internally and externally, and attend to these accounts at the proper time? Therefore, in the continuation of the verse, it adds: He is aware of all hidden matters, and not even the weight of an atom in all the heavens nor in the earth shall be outside the sphere of His infinite knowledge (عالِمِ الْغَيْبِ لا يَعْزُبُ عَنْهُ مِثْقالُ ذَرَّةٍ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ لا فِي الْأَرْضِ‌ [1]) "nor anything smaller than an atom, nor larger than it, but that all these are recorded and preserved in a Clear Book" (وَ لا أَصْغَرُ مِنْ ذلِكَ وَ لا أَكْبَرُ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ‌).

In this way, neither the scattering of the particles of human bodies in the earth, nor their mixing with other beings, nor even the entry of these parts into the bodies of other humans through food creates any problem for their restoration.

Their deeds also remain in this world, even if they change form, and He is well aware of all of them.

A similar expression has also appeared in Surah Qaf, verses 3 and 4, where it says: "What! When we are dead and have become dust (shall we be resurrected)? That is a far-off return. We already know what the earth diminishes of them, and with Us is a Preserving Book" (أَ إِذا مِتْنا وَ كُنَّا تُراباً ذلِكَ رَجْعٌ بَعِيدٌ قَدْ عَلِمْنا ما تَنْقُصُ الْأَرْضُ مِنْهُمْ وَ عِنْدَنا كِتابٌ حَفِيظٌ).

Regarding what is meant by the "Clear Book" (Kitab-un Mubin), many commentators have said:

It is the "Preserved Tablet" (Lawh-un Mahfuz). But the question arises: what is the "Preserved Tablet"?

As we have said before, the closest interpretation that can be given for the "Preserved Tablet" is the tablet of "the infinite knowledge of the Lord." Yes, in this tablet, everything is recorded and preserved, and no change or alteration finds its way into it.

The vast universe of existence is also a reflection of this Preserved Tablet, for all the particles of our being and all our words and deeds remain preserved in it; although they apparently change face, they never perish.

Then, within two verses, it states the purpose of the rising of the Resurrection, or in other words, it explains the reason for the necessity of such a world after the current world to the deniers, saying: "The goal is that He may reward those who have believed and done righteous deeds" (لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ‌). Yes, "for them is forgiveness and a noble provision" (أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَ رِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ‌).

If the righteous believers do not receive their reward, would not the principle of justice—which is among the most fundamental principles of creation—be suspended? Can the justice of the Lord have any meaning without that? While in this world we see many such individuals who never receive the recompense for their good deeds; therefore, there must be another world so that this principle may be realized there.

Placing "forgiveness" (maghfirah) before "noble provision" (rizq-un karim) may be because the greatest concern of the believers is regarding the slips they might have had; therefore, before anything else, He grants them peace of mind by stating His forgiveness. Furthermore, until they are washed with the water of divine forgiveness, they will not be worthy of "noble provision" and a noble station.

"Noble provision" means any valuable sustenance, and the breadth of its concept is such that it includes all divine blessings, even "bounties which no eye has seen, no ear has heard, and has not crossed the mind of any person."

In other words, Paradise with all its spiritual and material blessings is gathered in this word.

Although some commentators have interpreted "noble" (karim) as two things: good and trouble-free [1], it seems that its concept is even broader than this.

And since the other part of justice concerns the punishment of sinners and criminals, in the next verse it adds: "And those who strove to belie Our signs, seeking to frustrate and deny them, and imagined they could escape the sphere of Our power, for them there will be a punishment of the worst and most painful of torments" (وَ الَّذِينَ سَعَوْا فِي آياتِنا مُعاجِزِينَ أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ مِنْ رِجْزٍ أَلِيمٌ‌). There, the talk was of "noble provision," and here it is of "painful rijz."

"Rijz" (rhyming with kidhb) originally means "agitation and the inability to maintain balance." Therefore, when a camel becomes ill and weak and is forced to take short steps while moving so it can maintain its balance a little, the Arabs call this state "rijz." Subsequently, it was applied to any kind of sin and impurity.

The application of the word "rajaz" (rhyming with marad) to specific battle poems is also because they have short and closely spaced segments.

In any case, what is meant by "rijz" here is the worst types of punishment, which is also emphasized by the mention of the word "painful" (alim), and it includes various types of painful physical and spiritual punishments.

Some have noted that here, when stating the blessings of the people of Paradise, the Quran did not mention the word "from" (min), to indicate its vastness, but this word appeared regarding the punishment to be a sign of its relative limitation and an expression of His mercy.

"Sa'aw" is from the root "sa'y," meaning any kind of effort and striving, and here it means the effort to belie and deny the signs of truth and to prevent people from inclining toward the religion of the Lord.

"Mu'ajizin" is from the root "mu'ajazah," meaning to make someone unable, and in such cases, it is applied to those who flee from someone such that he cannot gain mastery over them. Obviously, this description for the criminals is because of the notion they practically demonstrated; their work resembled those who imagined they could commit any crime they wanted and then flee from the sphere of Allah's power.

[^1]: "Alusi" in "Ruh al-Ma'ani" under the verse in question. [1]: "Ya'zubu" is from the root "uzub," which originally means moving away from the family to find pasture, then it was applied to any kind of hiding or being absent. For this reason, men or women who have remained away from their spouses are called "azab" or "azabah" (unmarried/single).

Ayahs

وَيَرَى ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقَّ وَيَهْدِىٓ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَمِيدِ (٦)
Those who have been given knowledge see that what has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth and [that] it guides to the path of the All-mighty, the All-laudable. (6)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ هَلْ نَدُلُّكُمْ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ يُنَبِّئُكُمْ إِذَا مُزِّقْتُمْ كُلَّ مُمَزَّقٍ إِنَّكُمْ لَفِى خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ (٧)
The faithless say, ‘Shall we show you a man who will inform you [that] when you have been totally rent to pieces you will indeed have a new creation? (7)
أَفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَم بِهِۦ جِنَّةٌۢ ۗ بَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ وَٱلضَّلَـٰلِ ٱلْبَعِيدِ (٨)
Has he fabricated a lie against Allah, or is there a madness in him?’ Indeed, those who do not believe in the Hereafter languish in punishment and extreme error. (8)
أَفَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ إِن نَّشَأْ نَخْسِفْ بِهِمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ أَوْ نُسْقِطْ عَلَيْهِمْ كِسَفًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّكُلِّ عَبْدٍ مُّنِيبٍ (٩)
Have they not regarded that which is before them and that which is behind them of the sky and the earth? If We like, We can make the earth swallow them, or let a fragment from the sky fall on them. There is indeed a sign in that for every penitent servant. (9)

Commentary

The Scholars Recognize Your Call as the Truth

In the preceding verses, the discussion concerned the blind-hearted ignorant ones who adamantly denied the Resurrection despite all its proofs and strove to belie Divine signs and lead others astray.

In connection with that, the verses under discussion speak of the scholars and thinkers who testify to the truth of the Divine verses and encourage others to accept them. It says:

"And those who have been given knowledge see that what is revealed to you from your Lord is the truth, and it guides to the path of the Exalted, the Praiseworthy" (وَ يَرَى الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ هُوَ الْحَقَّ وَ يَهْدِي إِلى‌ صِراطِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَمِيدِ).

Some commentators have interpreted "those who have been given knowledge" (الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ) in this verse as referring to those scholars of the People of the Book who, upon witnessing the signs of the Quran's authenticity, humbled themselves before it and confessed to its truth.

While there is no objection to the scholars of the People of the Book being one of the instances of the above verse, there is no reason to limit it to them. Rather, considering the word "see" (يَرَى), which is a present-tense verb, and the broadness of the concept "those who have been given knowledge," it encompasses all scientists and thinkers in every age, time, and place.

And if we see in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim that this expression is interpreted as referring to the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, it is in fact stating the most perfect and complete instance of it.

Indeed, any unbiased scholar who reflects upon the content of this Heavenly Book—from its profound knowledge, firm laws, wise counsels, and moving exhortations to its lesson-bearing histories and miraculous scientific discussions—will know that they all testify to the truth of these verses.

Today, various books have been authored by Western and Eastern scholars regarding Islam and the Quran, in which very eloquent and clear admissions regarding the greatness of Islam and the truth of the above verse can be seen.

The expression "it is the truth" (هُوَ الْحَقَّ) is a comprehensive term that applies to the entire content of the Quran, because "Truth" (Haqq) is that very objective reality and external existence; meaning, the content of the Quran is harmonious with the laws of creation and the realities of the universe and the human world.

And because it is so, it guides toward the path of God—the God who is both "Exalted" (Aziz) and "Praiseworthy" (Hamid). That is, while being powerful and invincible, He is worthy of all praise, unlike human power-holders who, when they sit upon the throne of power, adopt the path of bullying, aggression, tyranny, and monopolization.

A similar expression appears in verse 1 of Surah Ibrahim, where it says:

"A Book which We have revealed to you that you may bring mankind out of darknesses into the light by the permission of their Lord—to the path of the Exalted, the Praiseworthy" (كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ إِلَيْكَ لِتُخْرِجَ النَّاسَ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهمْ إِلى‌ صِراطِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَمِيدِ).

It is evident that for One who is both powerful and worthy of praise, both All-Knowing and Aware, and Merciful and Compassionate, His path is the most reliable of paths and the straightest of ways. Those who walk His path draw themselves closer to the source of power and all praiseworthy attributes.

In the next verse, it returns once more to the issue of the Day of Judgment and Resurrection, completing the previous discussions in another form. It says: "The disbelievers said:

'Shall we point out to you a man who informs you that when you have been torn into every piece (and your body's particles have separated and each settled in a corner, or perhaps became part of the body of another animal or human), you will indeed be [raised] in a new creation'?" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا هَلْ نَدُلُّكُمْ عَلى‌ رَجُلٍ يُنَبِّئُكُمْ إِذا مُزِّقْتُمْ كُلَّ مُمَزَّقٍ إِنَّكُمْ لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ).

It seems their insistence on denying the Resurrection stemmed from two matters:

First, they imagined that the Resurrection the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله of Islam proposed (the physical Resurrection) was a vulnerable subject through which they could make the masses cynical and easily negate it.

Second, belief in the Resurrection, or even its probable acceptance, creates a sense of responsibility and commitment in a human being and compels him toward reflection and seeking the truth. This was considered extremely dangerous for the leaders of disbelief; hence, they insisted on removing the thought of the Resurrection and the reward and punishment of deeds from people's minds by any means possible.

They would say: Is it possible for these decayed bones, these scattered dust particles whose fragments the wind carries in every direction, to one day be gathered and clothed in the garment of life?

Furthermore, their use of the term "a man" (رجل) in an indefinite form to refer to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was intended as a slight.

But they had forgotten that in the beginning, we were also scattered parts; every drop of water in our bodies was in a corner of an ocean or a spring, and every particle of organic and mineral matter of our bodies lay in some corner of the earth. Just as Allah gathered these in the beginning, He has the power to do so at the end.

And surprisingly, they took this speech as evidence of the speaker's lying or madness, saying: "Has he invented a lie against Allah, or is there madness in him?" (أَفْتَرى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً أَمْ بِهِ جِنَّةٌ).

Otherwise, how could a truthful and sane person utter such words?! But the Quran answers them decisively: "Nay, he is neither mad nor a liar; rather, those who do not believe in the Hereafter are in torment and far-reaching error" (بَلِ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ فِي الْعَذابِ وَ الضَّلالِ الْبَعِيدِ).

What error is more manifest than for a person to deny the Resurrection—a Resurrection of which he sees examples every year before his eyes in the world of nature and the reviving of dead lands?

A Resurrection without which the life of this world would be meaningless and hollow.

And finally, a Resurrection whose denial is equivalent to denying the power, justice, and wisdom of the Lord.

But why does it say they are now in torment and error?

This is because there are difficulties and events in life that a person cannot endure without faith in the Hereafter.

Truly, if life were limited to these few days of worldly existence, the thought of death would be a terrifying nightmare for every human being. For this reason, deniers of the Resurrection always live in a kind of agonizing anxiety and painful torment, whereas believers in the Resurrection consider death a gateway to the world of eternity and a means to break the cage and be freed from this prison.

Yes, faith in the Resurrection grants peace to the human being, makes difficulties bearable, and makes altruism, sacrifice, and martyrdom easy for mankind.

Fundamentally, those who considered the Resurrection as evidence of lying or madness were, due to their disbelief and ignorance, caught in the torment of dark-sightedness and a long, far-reaching error.

Although some commentators have considered this "torment" as a reference to the punishment of the Hereafter, the literal meaning of the verse indicates that they are currently caught in torment and error in this world.

Then, it proceeds to another proof regarding the Resurrection, a proof coupled with a threat to the stubborn heedless ones, saying: "Have they not looked at what is before them and what is behind them of the heaven and the earth?" (أَ فَلَمْ يَرَوْا إِلى‌ ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ ما خَلْفَهُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌).

This magnificent sky with all these wonders, with all these fixed and wandering stars and the systems governing them, and likewise this earth with all its marvels and types of living creatures and blessings and bounties, is the most eloquent proof of the Creator's power.

Is One who has power over all these matters unable to return man to the world of life after death?! This is the "Argument of Power" which has been used as reasoning against the deniers of the Resurrection in other verses of the Quran, including at the end of Surah Ya-Sin (verse 82), verse 99 of Surah Al-Isra, and verses 6 and 7 of Surah Qaf.

Furthermore, this sentence is a prelude to threatening this prejudiced, dark-hearted group who insist on closing their eyes to all realities; hence, it follows by saying:

"If We willed, We could cause the earth to swallow them"—an earthquake could occur, the earth could split open, and they could be buried within it! (إِنْ نَشَأْ نَخْسِفْ بِهِمُ الْأَرْضَ‌).

"Or if We willed, We could cause fragments from the sky to fall upon them"—crushing them, their homes, and their lives (أَوْ نُسْقِطْ عَلَيْهِمْ كِسَفاً مِنَ السَّماءِ).

Indeed, "In that is surely a sign for every servant who turns [to Allah] and employs his thought and reflection" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِكُلِّ عَبْدٍ مُنِيبٍ‌).

Everyone has seen or heard of examples of earthquakes and land subsidences in their life, and has seen or heard of the fall of celestial stones from the upper atmosphere, or the collapse of mountains due to lightning or volcanoes and the like. Every rational person knows that the occurrence of these matters is possible at any moment and in any place. If the earth has tranquility and the sky is safe and secure for us, it is by the power and command of Another. We, who are held in the grip of His power from every side, how can we deny His ability regarding the Resurrection! Or how can we flee from the domain of His governance?

Key Points

  1. Although the sky is above and the earth is beneath the feet, the above verse uses the expression "what is before them" (ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ) and "what is behind them" (وَ ما خَلْفَهُمْ), and this is the only instance in the Quran where this specific phrasing is seen. This expression might refer to the fact that the view of the sky at the time of the rising and setting of the sun, moon, and stars is of greater importance, and the power and greatness of the Truth are more manifest at those moments. We know that when a person stands facing the horizon, this view is "before him," and the earth, which is of secondary importance, is introduced as being "behind him."

Furthermore, if these arrogant heedless ones do not even allow themselves to look above their heads, why do they not at least look at what is "before them" and seen along the horizon?!

  1. We know that the interior of the Earth is molten, scorching, and erupting, and in reality, the lives of all humans are situated upon a collection of potential volcanoes. A single small Divine command is enough for one of these volcanoes to emerge, shake a vast region, and pour stones and molten, scorching material upon it.

We also know that every twenty-four hours, hundreds of thousands of small and large wandering stones enter the Earth's gravitational field and are pulled toward it. If it were not for their collision with the layer of air surrounding the Earth—which causes them to heat up and burn—the inhabitants of the Earth would be constantly bombarded with stones from the sky. Even now, their power and intensity are such that they sometimes bypass this barrier and fall to the earth, and this is a Divine warning.

Therefore, if we humans live in the utmost tranquility between these two sources of danger by the command of Allah, is this not enough for us to realize His great power and lay our heads upon His threshold?!

  1. It is noteworthy that at the end of the last verse above, it states that in these things is a clear sign of the greatness and power of Allah, but for every "servant who turns in repentance" (بنده انابه كننده).

This indicates that the rebellious tyrants who have removed the collar of servitude to Allah from their necks, as well as the heedless servants who continue their incorrect and sinful path and for whom there is no turning back or repentance, will not benefit from these clear signs either. For the existence of the sun alone is not enough; a seeing eye and the removal of the veil are also necessary.

Ayahs

۞ وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا دَاوُۥدَ مِنَّا فَضْلًا ۖ يَـٰجِبَالُ أَوِّبِى مَعَهُۥ وَٱلطَّيْرَ ۖ وَأَلَنَّا لَهُ ٱلْحَدِيدَ (١٠)
Certainly We gave David our grace: ‘O mountains and birds, chime in with him!’ And We made iron soft for him, (10)
أَنِ ٱعْمَلْ سَـٰبِغَـٰتٍ وَقَدِّرْ فِى ٱلسَّرْدِ ۖ وَٱعْمَلُوا۟ صَـٰلِحًا ۖ إِنِّى بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (١١)
saying, ‘Make easy coats of mail, and keep the measure in arranging [the links], and act righteously. Indeed I watch what you do.’ (11)

Commentary

Allah’s Great Bounties upon David

Since the final verse of the previous discussion spoke of the "penitent servant" (abd munib), and we know that this description is mentioned in some Quranic verses (Surah Sad, 24) regarding the Prophet David (Dawud) عليه السلام—with details that will come by Allah’s will—it is most fitting to recount a portion of the life of this great Prophet and his son Solomon (Sulayman) عليه السلام as a model to complete the previous discussion.

Furthermore, it serves as a warning to all those who consign Allah’s blessings to oblivion and fail to be His servants when they sit upon the throne of power.

In the first verse, He says: "And certainly We gave David bounty from Us" (وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا دَاوُودَ مِنَّا فَضْلًا).

The word "bounty" (fadl) has a broad meaning that encompasses all the gifts Allah bestowed upon David, and its mention in the "indefinite" form (nakirah) is evidence of its greatness.

Prophet David was the recipient of many bounties from the Lord, both in spiritual and material aspects, as attested by the verses of the Quran.

In one place, He says: "And We certainly gave David and Solomon knowledge, and they said, 'Praise be to Allah, who has favored us over many of His believing servants'" (وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا دَاوُودَ وَسُلَيْمَانَ عِلْمًا وَقَالَا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي فَضَّلَنَا عَلَىٰ كَثِيرٍ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ) (An-Naml: 15).

In another place, emphasis is specifically placed on the knowledge of the speech of animals, presented as a great virtue: "O mankind, we have been taught the language of birds, and we have been given from all things. Indeed, this is evident bounty" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ عُلِّمْنَا مَنْطِقَ الطَّيْرِ وَأُوتِينَا مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَهُوَ الْفَضْلُ الْمُبِينُ) (An-Naml: 16). The various miracles mentioned in the verse under discussion are part of those virtues. Additionally, his exceptionally beautiful voice and melody, and the power of just judgment mentioned in Surah Sad, are considered other parts of this divine bounty. Most importantly was the virtue of prophethood and messengership that Allah placed at David's disposal.

In any case, after this brief allusion, He proceeds to detail it, describing a portion of David's spiritual and material virtues as follows: "We said: O mountains, repeat [Our] praises with him, and [so did] the birds" (يَا جِبَالُ أَوِّبِي مَعَهُ وَالطَّيْرَ). Whenever he engaged in the remembrance and glorification of Allah, they would join in the chant.

The word awwibi (أوّبي) originates from ta'wib, meaning the echoing and modulation of the voice in the throat. This root is sometimes used to mean "repentance" (tawbah) because its reality is a "return" to Allah.

Although every particle in the universe glorifies and praises Allah—whether a David joins them or not—David's distinction was that when he raised his voice and began the melody of glorification, what was latent within these beings became manifest, and the internal murmur turned into an external melody. This is similar to what has been narrated regarding the glorification of the "pebbles" in the hand of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "He went out reciting the Psalms (Zabur), and whenever he recited the Psalms, there remained no mountain, no stone, and no bird but that it responded to him!" [1]

After mentioning this spiritual virtue, He mentions a material virtue, saying: "And We made iron soft for him" (وَأَلَنَّا لَهُ الْحَدِيدَ).

It might be said that Allah, in a miraculous manner, taught David the method of softening iron so that he could create thin and strong wires from it for weaving coats of mail. Or it might be said that before David, iron plates were used for defense in wars, which were both heavy and, if worn, so rigid and inflexible that they caused great discomfort to the warriors. No one until that day had been able to weave something like a coat of mail from thin, strong iron wires that could be worn easily like clothing and remain soft and fluid with the body's movements.

However, the apparent meaning of the verse is that the softening of iron in David's hand occurred by Divine command and in a miraculous form. What is to prevent the One who gave the hot furnace the property of softening iron from placing this same property in another form within David's palms? Some Islamic narrations also point to this meaning. [1]

In a hadith, it is mentioned that Allah sent a revelation to David: "What an excellent servant you are, except that you eat from the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal)." David wept for forty mornings (asking Allah for a solution). Then Allah softened iron for him, and he would make a coat of mail every day... and thus he became independent of the public treasury. [2]

It is true that the public treasury is for the consumption of those who provide service to society without compensation and carry important communal burdens, but how much better it is if a person can both provide this service and—if capable—earn a living through the labor of his own hands. David desired to be such an exceptional servant.

In any case, David used this ability given to him by Allah in the best way—namely, making tools for Jihad, specifically tools for protection against the enemy—and he never utilized it for the ordinary tools of life. Furthermore, from its income—according to some narrations—besides managing his own simple life, he would also spend on the needy. [1] In addition to all this, another benefit of this work was that it served as a living miracle for him.

Some commentators have narrated that Luqman came to David while he was weaving the first coat of mail. He was turning the iron into numerous wires and rings and interlocking them. This sight greatly captured Luqman's attention, and he fell into thought, yet he continued to watch and asked no questions until David finished weaving the armor. David stood up, put it on, and said, "What an excellent means of defense for war!" Luqman, having realized the ultimate purpose, said: "Silence is wisdom, but few are those who practice it!" [2]

The next verse is an elaboration on David's armor-making and the Lord's very meaningful command in this regard. It says: "Make full coats of mail and measure the links precisely" (أَنِ اعْمَلْ سَابِغَاتٍ وَقَدِّرْ فِي السَّرْدِ).

Sabighat is the plural of sabigh, meaning a complete and ample coat of mail; "the completion of a blessing" (isbagh al-ni'mah) also means the ampleness of a blessing.

Sard originally means the weaving of coarse objects like armor. The phrase "measure the links precisely" (qaddir fi al-sard) means observing proportionate measurements in the rings of the armor and the manner of its weaving.

In reality, Allah gives David an instruction that should be a model for all faithful craftsmen and workers of the world: the instruction of robust workmanship and the observance of precision in quality and quantity in manufactured goods, such that consumers can use them well and comfortably, and they possess complete strength.

He says to David: Make the armor wide and comfortable so that the warrior is not trapped as if in a prison when wearing it. Do not make the rings excessively small and thin such that they lose their flexibility, nor too large and misshapen such that the tip of a sword, dagger, spear, or arrow might pass through. Let everything be proportionate and measured.

In short: Allah provided both the "raw material" by virtue of "We made iron soft for him" and taught him the "form" and method of constructing the armor, so that a perfect product would be produced from this "matter" and "form."

At the end of the verse, He addresses David and his family, saying: "And work righteousness; indeed, I am Seeing of what you do" (وَاعْمَلُوا صَالِحًا إِنِّي بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ).

The addressee at the beginning of the verse is David alone, but at the end, it is him and his family or his people, because all these matters are a prelude to righteous action. The goal is not merely making armor or earning income; the goal is righteous action, and these are means on that path which both David and his family utilized.

One of the aspects of righteous action is to observe sufficient precision in industries from every angle and to present a complete and useful product, avoiding any poor workmanship or negligence.

It is also possible that the addressees of this command were David and all those who used his handiwork, implying that they should use this means of defense in the way of righteous action, not in the way of oppression, injustice, or sin.

[1] Kamal al-Din by Saduq (as cited in Al-Mizan, Vol. 16, p. 390). [1] Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 3, p. 343, and Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 315. [2] Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed. [1] Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, Vol. 9, p. 192. [2] Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed.

Ayahs

وَلِسُلَيْمَـٰنَ ٱلرِّيحَ غُدُوُّهَا شَهْرٌ وَرَوَاحُهَا شَهْرٌ ۖ وَأَسَلْنَا لَهُۥ عَيْنَ ٱلْقِطْرِ ۖ وَمِنَ ٱلْجِنِّ مَن يَعْمَلُ بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِۦ ۖ وَمَن يَزِغْ مِنْهُمْ عَنْ أَمْرِنَا نُذِقْهُ مِنْ عَذَابِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (١٢)
And for Solomon [We subjected] the wind: its morning course was a month’s journey and its evening course was a month’s journey. We made a fount of [molten] copper flow for him, and [We placed at his service] some of the jinn who would work for him by the permission of his Lord, and if any of them swerved from Our command, We would make him taste the punishment of the Blaze. (12)
يَعْمَلُونَ لَهُۥ مَا يَشَآءُ مِن مَّحَـٰرِيبَ وَتَمَـٰثِيلَ وَجِفَانٍ كَٱلْجَوَابِ وَقُدُورٍ رَّاسِيَـٰتٍ ۚ ٱعْمَلُوٓا۟ ءَالَ دَاوُۥدَ شُكْرًا ۚ وَقَلِيلٌ مِّنْ عِبَادِىَ ٱلشَّكُورُ (١٣)
They built for him as many temples as he wished, and figures, basins like cisterns, and caldrons fixed [in the ground]. ‘O House of David, act thankfully, and few of My servants are grateful.’ (13)
فَلَمَّا قَضَيْنَا عَلَيْهِ ٱلْمَوْتَ مَا دَلَّهُمْ عَلَىٰ مَوْتِهِۦٓ إِلَّا دَآبَّةُ ٱلْأَرْضِ تَأْكُلُ مِنسَأَتَهُۥ ۖ فَلَمَّا خَرَّ تَبَيَّنَتِ ٱلْجِنُّ أَن لَّوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ ٱلْغَيْبَ مَا لَبِثُوا۟ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْمُهِينِ (١٤)
When We decreed death for him, nothing apprised them of his death except a worm which gnawed away at his staff. And when he fell down, [the humans] realized that had the jinn known the Unseen, they would not have remained in a humiliating torment. (14)

Commentary

The Grandeur of Sulayman and His Exemplary Death

Following the discussion of the blessings bestowed upon Dawud, the discourse turns to his son, Sulayman (peace be upon him). While two blessings were mentioned regarding Dawud, three great blessings are discussed concerning his son Sulayman. He says: "And to Sulayman [We subjected] the wind; its morning journey was a month’s distance, and its evening journey was a month’s distance!" (وَ لِسُلَيْمانَ الرِّيحَ غُدُوُّها شَهْرٌ وَ رَواحُها شَهْرٌ) [^1]. It is noteworthy that for the father, He subjected a coarse and exceptionally solid substance—iron—while for the son, He subjected a very subtle entity; yet both were constructive, miraculous, and beneficial. He softened the hard solid for Dawud and made the subtle waves of the wind active and powerful for Sulayman! The subtlety of the wind never prevents it from performing significant tasks; it is the winds

[^1]: "Li-Sulayman" (to Sulayman) is a prepositional phrase related to a hidden verb, namely "Sakhkharna" (We subjected), which is understood by the context of the preceding verses and explicitly stated in Surah Sad, verse 36: "So We subjected the wind to him" (فَسَخَّرْنا لَهُ الرِّيحَ). Some commentators believe the "Lam" in "Li-Sulayman" denotes exclusivity, indicating that this miracle was specific to this Prophet and no other Prophet shared it with him.

that move massive ships across the oceans, turn heavy millstones, and propel balloons through the sky like aircraft.

Indeed, Allah placed this subtle substance with such staggering power at the disposal of Sulayman.

How the wind moved Sulayman’s apparatus (his throne or carpet) is not clear to us; we only know that nothing is difficult or complex before the power of Allah. If man, with his meager power, can move balloons (chambers filled with light gases that fly into the sky, sometimes carrying people) and today moves giant, colossal airplanes with hundreds of passengers and vast equipment in the heights of the heavens, how could moving Sulayman’s carpet by means of the wind be difficult for Allah?

What factor protected Sulayman and his apparatus from falling, or from air pressure and other challenges arising from celestial movement?! This too is among the matters whose details are not clear to us, but we know that such extraordinary events (miracles) have been frequent in the history of the Prophets, even though, unfortunately, ignorant individuals or clever enemies have mixed them with superstitions that have distorted and marred the true face of these matters. We content ourselves with the extent mentioned by the Quran [^1]. "Ghuduw" (on the pattern of 'uluw) means the morning time, as opposed to "Rawah," which refers to the evening time when animals return to their resting places. However, it appears from the context of the verse under discussion that "Ghuduw" means

[^1]: We have also discussed this in Volume 13, page 472 onwards (under verse 81 of Surah Al-Anbiya).

the first half of the day, and "Rawah" the second half. The meaning of the verse is that Sulayman, from morning until noon, would travel with this swift mount a distance equivalent to a month's journey for travelers of that time, and in the second half of the day, he would travel the same distance.

Then, He points to the second divine blessing upon Sulayman, saying: "And We made a spring of [molten] copper flow for him!" (وَ أَسَلْنا لَهُ عَيْنَ الْقِطْرِ).

"Asalna" is from the root "Sayalan," meaning to make flow, and "Qitr" means copper. The meaning is that We melted this metal for him, and it became like a flowing spring of water! Some have interpreted "Qitr" as various types of metals or as "zinc," and thus for the father, iron became soft, and for the son, metals were melted (though the first meaning is the well-known one).

How was a spring of molten copper or other metals placed at Sulayman’s disposal? Did Allah teach this Prophet the method of melting these metals on a very vast and extensive scale through miracle and inspiration?

Or was a spring of this liquid metal—similar to the springs that flow from the slopes of volcanoes during activity—placed at his disposal in a miraculous manner? Or in some other way? It is not precisely clear to us; we only know that this too was among the divine favors upon this great Prophet.

Finally, He proceeds to state the third blessing of the Lord upon Sulayman, which is the subjection of a vast group of Jinn, saying: "And of the Jinn were those who worked before him by the permission of his Lord." (وَ مِنَ الْجِنِّ مَنْ يَعْمَلُ بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ‌).

"And whoever of them deviated from Our command, We made him taste of the punishment of the Blazing Fire."

(وَ مَنْ يَزِغْ مِنْهُمْ عَنْ أَمْرِنا نُذِقْهُ مِنْ عَذابِ السَّعِيرِ).

"Jinn," as its name implies, is a being hidden from the senses, possessing intellect and power, and as understood from the verses of the Quran, is bound by divine obligations.

Many legends and superstitious stories have been fabricated about the "Jinn," but if we remove these superstitions, the reality of their existence and the specific attributes mentioned for them in the Quran are matters that never contradict science or reason. We will provide a more detailed explanation of this subject in the commentary of "Surah Al-Jinn," Allah willing.

In any case, it is understood from the phrasing of the above verse that the subjection of this massive force was also by the command of the Lord, and whenever they refused to perform their duties, they were punished.

A group of commentators has said that "the punishment of the Blazing Fire" (عَذابِ السَّعِيرِ) here refers to the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, whereas the apparent meaning of the verse refers to the punishment of the defiant in this world. It is also clearly understood from the verses of "Surah Sad" that Allah had placed a group of devils at his disposal who performed important construction works for him, and whenever they defied him, he would bind them in chains: "And [also] the devils [of Jinn]—every builder and diver, and others bound together in shackles" (Sad, verses 37 and 38). It is noteworthy that to manage a vast government and a sprawling country like Sulayman’s, many factors are necessary, but the most important are three factors alluded to in the above verse:

First, a swift, continuous, and comprehensive means of transport so that the head of government can be informed of all aspects of his country.

Second, raw materials to be used for preparing the necessary tools for the people's lives and various industries.

And finally, an active labor force that can make sufficient use of those materials, give them the necessary quality, and meet the various needs of the country in this regard.

And we see that Allah placed all three of these matters at Sulayman’s disposal, and he, in turn, utilized them in the best possible way for the welfare of the people, development, prosperity, and security.

This subject is not exclusive to the era of Sulayman and his government; paying attention to it is necessary today, tomorrow, here, and everywhere for the correct administration of countries.

In the next verse, He refers to a part of the important production works of the group of Jinn that they performed by Sulayman’s command, saying: "They made for him whatever he desired of sanctuaries, statues, basins like reservoirs, and fixed cooking-pots." (يَعْمَلُونَ لَهُ ما يَشاءُ مِنْ مَحارِيبَ وَ تَماثِيلَ وَ جِفانٍ كَالْجَوابِ وَ قُدُورٍ راسِياتٍ‌).

Some of these were related to spiritual and devotional matters, and some were proportionate to the physical needs of the people and the vast population of his soldiers and officials.

"Maharib" is the plural of "Mihrab," which linguistically means "place of worship" or "large palaces and buildings" constructed for the purpose of being a sanctuary.

Sometimes it is also applied to the front part of a gathering, or the front of a mosque or temple. What is called a "Mihrab" today—the place where the Imam of the congregation stands—is actually a newer expression and meaning derived from the original root.

In any case, since this word is from the root "Harb" meaning war, the reason for naming temples "Mihrab" is said to be that they are places of "warfare" and battle against Satan and carnal desires [^1], or from "Harb" meaning the garment taken from the enemy's body on the battlefield, because a person in a temple must strip themselves of the covering of worldly thoughts and distractions [^2]. In any case, these active and agile workers of Sulayman prepared large and magnificent temples for him, worthy of his divine and religious government, so that people could easily perform their devotional duties.

"Tamathil" is the plural of "Timthal," which means both patterns/images and statues. Regarding what beings these statues or images represented and for what purpose Sulayman ordered their preparation, various interpretations have been given:

It is possible these were for decorative purposes, as seen in important ancient and even modern buildings.

Or they were to increase the awe of his establishment, as the depiction of certain animals like lions creates a sense of awe in the minds of many people.

Was the sculpting of animate beings permissible in the law of Sulayman (peace be upon him), even though it is forbidden in Islam? Or were the statues made for Sulayman of inanimate types, such as depictions of trees, mountains, the sun, the moon, and the stars?

Or did they only make patterns and engravings on the walls, which are frequently seen in the delicate craftsmanship of ancient monuments? We know that patterns and engravings, whatever they may be, are not forbidden, unlike statues.

[^1] and [^2]: Raghib's "Mufradat," root "Harb."

All of these are possible, for it is possible that the prohibition of sculpting in Islam was for the purpose of a fierce struggle against the issue of idolatry and its eradication, and this necessity did not exist to this extent in the time of Sulayman, and this ruling was not in his law.

However, in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the interpretation of this verse, we read:

"By Allah, they were not statues of men and women, but rather trees and the like." [^1] According to this narration, the sculpting of animate beings was also forbidden in his law.

"Jifan" is the plural of "Jifnah" (on the pattern of 'waznah'), meaning large bowls or platters for eating.

And "Jawab" is the plural of "Jabiyah," meaning a water basin. From this expression, it is understood that very massive eating vessels, each like a basin, were prepared for Sulayman so that a large group could sit around them and eat. If we recall, even in relatively recent times, large communal platters were used at tables for collective eating; in fact, their "table" was that large vessel itself, and individual, separate plates like today were not common.

"Qudur" is the plural of "Qidr" (on the pattern of 'qishr'), meaning a pot in which food is cooked, and "Rasiyat" is the plural of "Rasiyah," meaning firm and fixed. Here, it refers to pots that were so massive they were not moved from their places.

At the end of the verse, after mentioning these blessings, Allah addresses the household of Prophet Dawud, saying: "Work, O family of Dawud, in gratitude." (اعْمَلُوا آلَ داوُدَ شُكْراً).

"But few of My servants are grateful!" (وَ قَلِيلٌ مِنْ عِبادِيَ الشَّكُورُ). It is obvious that if gratitude meant only verbal thanks, it would not be a difficult matter such that those who practice it are few; rather, what is meant is "gratitude in action," which means using blessings in the path of the very goals for which they were created and bestowed. It is certain that those who use divine blessings generally in their proper place are but a few.

Some scholars have identified three stages for gratitude: gratitude with the heart, which is the conception of the blessing and feeling satisfaction and pleasure toward it;

Gratitude with the tongue, which is praising the Bestower of the blessing;

And gratitude with the other limbs and organs, which is harmonizing one's actions with that blessing.

"Shakur" is an intensive form (Sighah al-Mubalaghah) and denotes an abundance of gratitude, which is the repetition of thanks and its continuity with the heart, tongue, and limbs.

Of course, sometimes this attribute is also applied to Allah, such as what appears in Surah At-Taghabun, verse 17: "And Allah is Most Appreciative (Shakur) and Forbearing." Allah’s being "Shakur" means that to the extent His servants walk in the path of His obedience, He encompasses them with His blessings and favors, appreciates them, and adds to them from His grace more than they deserve.

In any case, the expression "few of My servants are grateful" may be to state the greatness of the status of this group who are exemplary individuals, or for the purpose that you should strive to be among them so that the number of the grateful may increase.

The last verse under discussion, which is also the final word concerning "Sulayman,"

speaks of the strange and exemplary death of this great Prophet of Allah, and clarifies the reality of how a Prophet of such greatness, and a ruler of such power and awe, easily surrendered his soul to the Creator, and even before his body could rest in bed, the claws of death seized him! He says: "When We decreed death for Sulayman, nothing indicated his death to them except a creature of the earth that ate his staff" until the staff broke and Sulayman’s body fell (فَلَمَّا قَضَيْنا عَلَيْهِ الْمَوْتَ ما دَلَّهُمْ عَلى‌ مَوْتِهِ إِلَّا دَابَّةُ الْأَرْضِ تَأْكُلُ مِنْسَأَتَهُ‌) [^1]. From the phrasing of the above verse and also numerous narrations, it is understood that when Sulayman’s death arrived, he was standing and leaning on his staff. Suddenly, death seized him and the soul departed from his body. He remained standing in that state for a period until the termite—which the Quran refers to as "Dabbat al-Ard" (creature of the earth)—ate his staff, causing him to lose his balance and fall to the ground, whereby the people became aware of his death! Therefore, He adds afterward: "When he fell, the Jinn realized that if they had known the unseen, they would not have remained in the humiliating punishment." (فَلَمَّا خَرَّ تَبَيَّنَتِ الْجِنُّ أَنْ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ الْغَيْبَ ما لَبِثُوا فِي الْعَذابِ الْمُهِينِ‌).

The word "Tabayyanat" from the root "Tabayyun" usually means to become manifest (intransitive verb) and sometimes means to know or become aware of something (transitive verb). Here, the second meaning is appropriate; that is, until that time, the group of Jinn were not aware of Sulayman’s death and they realized that if they had been aware of the secrets of the unseen, they would not have remained in the toil and hardship of heavy labor during this period.

A group of commentators has taken the sentence in the first sense and said the meaning of the verse is that after Sulayman fell, the condition of the Jinn became manifest to the humans—that they do not know the secrets of the unseen, and some had held such a belief about them without reason [^1]. The expression "humiliating punishment" (عَذابِ الْمُهِينِ) may refer to the heavy and difficult tasks that Sulayman sometimes assigned to a group of Jinn as a penalty or punishment; otherwise, a Prophet of Allah would never place anyone under pressure and punishment, let alone "humiliating punishment," without cause.

Key Points

1- A Perspective on the Exemplary Life of Sulayman (peace be upon him)

The Holy Quran—unlike the current Torah, which presents Sulayman (peace be upon him) as a tyrannical king, a builder of idol temples, and one surrendered to the whims of women [^2]—considers Sulayman (peace be upon him) a great Prophet of Allah. It presents him as a symbol of unparalleled power and governance, and within the discussions related to Sulayman, it has given great lessons to humanity, which was the primary goal of mentioning these stories.

In the above verses, we read that Allah gave great blessings to this great Prophet: a very swift and fast mount with which he could traverse his entire vast country in a short time;

Abundant mineral materials for various industries;

Sufficient active force to shape these mineral materials.

By utilizing these means, he built great temples and encouraged people to worship, and he also organized a vast and extensive program for hosting the soldiers, government employees, and the downtrodden masses of the people, the rest of which can be inferred from the examples of his vessels mentioned in the verses above.

In return for all these blessings, He commanded him to be grateful, emphasizing that very few can fulfill the right of gratitude for Allah’s blessings! Then He made clear how a man with such power and grandeur was vulnerable and helpless before death, passing away in a moment of sudden death, such that his end did not even give him the chance to sit or lie in bed. This was so that the arrogant and rebellious would not imagine that if they reached a position and acquired power, they had actually become powerful—he for whom Jinn and men, Satan and fairy were servants, and for whom the earth and sky were a playground, and in whose majesty anyone who doubted "the birds and fish would laugh at his intellect and thought"! In one short moment, he vanished like a bubble on the waves of the sea! And also to clarify how a meager staff kept him standing for a period, and observing his sitting or standing posture, the Jinn were busily working in the heat of the moment?

And also how a termite threw him to the ground and disrupted all the affairs of his country. Yes, one staff had set the active force of a vast country into motion, and one termite brought it to a halt!

Interestingly, it is mentioned in some narrations that on that day, Sulayman saw a handsome and well-dressed youth emerge from one of the corners of the palace and move toward him. Sulayman was surprised and said: "Who are you? And by whose permission did you come here?! I had ordered that no one should come here today!" He replied: "I am one who fears no kings! Nor do I take bribes!"—Sulayman was even more surprised—but he gave no chance and added: "I am the Angel of Death; I have come to take your soul!" He said this and immediately took his soul! [^1] We must also remind that the story of Sulayman, like many stories of the Prophets, has unfortunately been mixed with fabricated narrations, and superstitions have been attached to it that have distorted the face of this great Prophet. Many of these superstitions are taken from the current Torah, and if we content ourselves with what the Quran has said, no problem will arise.

2- Why was Sulayman’s death kept hidden for a period?

How long Sulayman’s death remained hidden from his government workers is not precisely clear. A year? A month? Or a few days?

Commentators do not have a single opinion on this.

Was this concealment done by those around him who knowingly kept his death hidden so that the affairs of the country would not temporarily collapse?

Or were those around him also unaware of this matter?!

It seems very unlikely that for a long period, even more than a day, those around him would not become aware, because certainly, some people were commissioned to bring him food and other necessities; they would have become aware of this event. Therefore, it is not unlikely—as some commentators have said—that they became aware of this matter but kept it hidden for certain interests. Thus, it is mentioned in some narrations that during this period, "Asaph bin Barkhiya," his special minister, managed the affairs of the country.

Was Sulayman standing and leaning on his staff, or sitting with his hands on the staff and his head resting on his hands when his soul was taken and he remained in that state for a while? Various possibilities exist, though the latter possibility seems closer.

If this period was long, did not the lack of eating and drinking raise a question for the observers?

Since everything about Sulayman was extraordinary, perhaps they considered this also among his wonders. We even read in a narration that gradually a murmur arose among a group that Sulayman should be worshipped; is it not that he has remained fixed in his place for a period, neither sleeping, nor eating, nor drinking? [^1] But when the staff broke and he fell, all these threads were severed, and their imaginations were shattered.

In any case, whatever it was, this delay in announcing Sulayman’s death revealed many things:

1- It became clear to everyone that even if a human reaches the peak of power, he is still a weak being before events, and is like a straw in the path of a storm, tossed in every direction.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), says in one of the sermons of Nahj al-Balagha:

"If anyone could find a ladder to eternal life or a way to repel death, it would have been Sulayman ibn Dawud (peace be upon him), for whom the kingdom of Jinn and men was subjected along with Prophethood and great proximity [to Allah]." [^1]

2- It became clear to all that the group of Jinn do not know the unseen, and the ignorant and unaware humans who worshipped them were gravely mistaken and in error.

3- For all people, this truth was revealed: how it is possible for the system and fabric of a country to depend on a small matter, to stand because of it, and to collapse with its fall, and how the infinite power of the Lord manifests beyond these matters.

3- The Image of Sulayman in the Quran and the Current Torah

While the Quran calls Sulayman a great Prophet with abundant knowledge and great piety—a Prophet who, despite having a massive government, was never enslaved by status or wealth, and said to those who had brought very precious gifts from the Queen of Sheba to entice him: "Do you provide me with wealth? But what Allah has given me is better than what He has given you" (An-Naml, 36).

A Prophet whose entire wish was to be able to fulfill the gratitude for the blessings of the Lord: "He said, 'My Lord, inspire me to be grateful for Your favor which You have bestowed upon me and upon my parents...'" (An-Naml, 19).

A leader who did not even allow anyone to knowingly wrong an ant; thus, in the Valley of the Ants, an ant called out: "O ants, enter your dwellings that you not be crushed by Sulayman and his soldiers while they perceive not!" (An-Naml, 18).

He was a worshipper who, whenever he became occupied with the world for a moment and distracted from the remembrance of Allah, would seek to compensate, saying: "Indeed, I gave preference to the love of good [things] over the remembrance of my Lord" (Sad, 32).

He was a sage who, despite his power, spoke only with logic, and even in conversation with a bird like the Hoopoe, did not lose sight of truth and justice.

He was a ruler whose deputy was so filled with "knowledge of the Book" that he could bring the throne of Bilqis in an instant.

And the Quran has described him with attributes such as "Awwab" (oft-returning to Allah) and "Ni'm al-'Abd" (an excellent servant).

One to whom Allah had given "governance" and "knowledge," and one whom He had encompassed with His guidance, and one who did not associate partners with Allah for a single moment in his life.

Yet, despite this, let us see how the current corrupted Torah stains the pure mantle of this great Prophet with polytheism and other things.

The Torah attributes the worst things to him regarding the building of idol temples, the promotion of idolatry, and excessive love for women, using very offensive expressions in describing his romantic pursuits, all of which are shameful to recount. We content ourselves with only one part that seems milder.

In the First Book of Kings, we read:

"But King Solomon loved many foreign women, as well as the daughter of Pharaoh: women of the Moabites, Ammonites, Edomites, Sidonians, and Hittites—from the nations of whom the Lord had said to the children of Israel, 'You shall not intermarry with them, nor they with you. Surely they will turn away your hearts after their gods.' Solomon clung to these in love. And he had seven hundred wives, princesses, and three hundred concubines; and his wives turned away his heart. For it was so, when Solomon was old, that his wives turned his heart after other gods; and his heart was not loyal to the Lord his God, as was the heart of his father David. For Solomon went after Ashtoreth the goddess of the Sidonians, and after Milcom the abomination of the Ammonites. Solomon did evil in the sight of the Lord, and did not fully follow the Lord, as did his father David. Then Solomon built a high place for Chemosh the abomination of Moab, on the hill that is east of Jerusalem, and for Molech the abomination of the people of Ammon... So the Lord became angry with Solomon, because his heart had turned from the Lord God of Israel, who had appeared to him twice... Therefore the Lord said to Solomon, 'Because you have done this, and have not kept My covenant and My statutes, which I have commanded you, I will surely tear the kingdom away from you and give it to your servant. Nevertheless I will not do it in your days, for the sake of your father David; I will tear it out of the hand of your son... However I will not tear away the whole kingdom; I will give one tribe to your son for the sake of My servant David, whom I have chosen, because he kept My commandments and My statutes! I will make him ruler all the days of his life...'" [^1]

From the sum of this false story in the Torah, it follows that:

1- Sulayman had a great affinity for women of idol-worshipping tribes, and contrary to Allah’s command, he took a large number of them, and gradually became inclined toward their religion! Even though he was not a "man who had not seen women," having 700 permanent wives and 300 concubines! His intense love for women led him away from the path of Allah! (We seek refuge in Allah).

2- Sulayman explicitly ordered the building of idol temples, and on the mountain facing "Jerusalem"—that holy center of Israel—he built a temple for the idol "Chemosh," the famous idol of the Moabite tribe, and the idol "Molech"—the specific idol of the Ammonite tribe—and he also developed a special affinity for the idol "Ashtoreth," the idol of the Sidonians. All of this happened in his old age!

3- Allah, because of this deviation and great sin, decreed a punishment for him, and that punishment was to take his country from him—but not from his own hand, rather from the hand of his son "Rehoboam"! And He would give him respite to reign as long as he wished; this too was for the sake of Allah’s special servant Dawud, Sulayman’s father—the same special servant of Allah who, according to the explicit text of the Torah (Allah forbid), had committed murder, adultery with a married woman, and the seizure of the wife of his own gallant officer and servant!!

Can anyone attribute these unjust accusations to the holy presence of a man like Sulayman?! If we consider Sulayman—as the Quran says—a Prophet, the situation is clear; and even if we consider him among the kings of the Children of Israel, such attributions cannot possibly be true of him.

For even if we do not consider him a Prophet, he was certainly next to a Prophet, as two books from the Old Testament, one named "Proverbs of Solomon" (or Wisdom of Solomon) and the other named "Song of Solomon," are among the sayings of this great man of God.

Truly, what answer do the Jews and Christians who believe in the current Torah have for these questions? And how do they accept these scandals?!

4- The Truly Grateful are Few

Before anything else, it is necessary to pay attention to the original root of the word "Shukr" (gratitude) in this regard:

Raghib says in "Al-Mufradat": "Shukr" is the conception of a blessing and its manifestation. Some have said it was originally "Kashr" meaning "Kashf" (to uncover), then it was transposed to become "Shukr." Its opposite is "Kufr," which is the forgetting of a blessing and its concealment.

Then he proceeds to divide "Shukr" into three branches: "Gratitude of the heart," which is thinking about the blessing; "Gratitude of the tongue," which is praising the Bestower; and "Gratitude of the other limbs," which is appreciation and responding to the blessing.

The Quranic expression in the above verses, "Work, O family of Dawud, in gratitude," shows that gratitude belongs more to the category of "action," and it must be presented within the fabric of human deeds. Perhaps for this reason, the Quran has considered the number of truly grateful people to be few. In addition to the above verses, in Surah Al-Mulk, verse 23, after enumerating great blessings such as the creation of hearing, sight, and hearts, it adds: "Little is the gratitude you give" (قَلِيلًا ما تَشْكُرُونَ). In Surah An-Naml, verse 73, it also says: "But most of them are not grateful" (وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَشْكُرُونَ). This is on one hand.

On the other hand, considering that Allah’s blessings, which surround the human being from head to toe, are so numerous that they cannot be numbered or calculated—as the Quran says: "And if you should count the favor of Allah, you could not enumerate them" (Ibrahim, 34)—it becomes clear why gratitude in its true sense for all blessings, such that one uses all of them without exception in the path of servitude to Allah (for which the blessings were created), is rarely found.

In other words, and as stated by some great commentators, "absolute gratitude" is that a person always remembers Allah without any forgetfulness, walks in His path without any sin, and obeys His command free from any defiance. It is certain that these attributes are combined in very few people. The idea that some have considered it fundamentally impossible is baseless and is evidence of their lack of familiarity with these concepts and these stages of servitude [^1].

It is sometimes said: fulfilling the right of gratitude to the Lord is very difficult from one perspective, because as soon as a person proceeds to give thanks and is granted this success and the means of gratitude are placed at his disposal, this itself is a new blessing that requires a new gratitude, and this subject continues in a sequence. The more a person strives in the path of thanking Him, the more he is encompassed by further blessings for which he is unable to give thanks! However, considering that one of the ways of fulfilling the right of divine gratitude is the very expression of inability to fulfill His gratitude, it becomes clear that a few of the Lord’s servants—as the Quran has stated—truly place themselves in this path.

Paying attention to the following hadiths can be sufficiently enlightening in this discussion:

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), we read: "Someone asked: Does the gratitude of the Lord have a limit such that if a person reaches it, he is considered grateful? He said: Yes. He asked: How? He said:

'He praises Allah for every blessing upon him regarding family and wealth, and if there is a right in the wealth Allah has given him, he fulfills it.'" [^1]

In another hadith from the same Imam, we read:

"Gratitude for the blessing is the avoidance of prohibited things." [^2]

And also in another hadith from the same Imam (peace be upon him), it is narrated that he said:

"Among what Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, revealed to Musa was: 'O Musa! Thank Me with the gratitude due to Me.' He said: 'O Lord! How can I thank You with the gratitude due to You when there is no thanks I give You except that You have bestowed it as a blessing upon me?' He said: 'O Musa! Now you have thanked Me, since you have known that that [success] is from Me!'" [^3]

It is also necessary to note that thanking and appreciating those who are the means of a blessing for a person is also a branch of thanking Allah, as Imam al-Sajjad, Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him), says: "When the Day of Resurrection comes, Allah the Exalted will say to some of His servants: 'Did you thank so-and-so?' He will say: 'O Lord! I thanked You.' Allah will say: 'Since you did not thank him, you have not thanked Me!'"

Then he added:

"The most grateful of you to Allah is the one who is most appreciative and grateful for the favors and efforts of the people." [^1]

Regarding the reality of "Shukr" (gratitude) and how it causes the increase of blessings, and how "Kufr" (ingratitude) causes their destruction, we have provided a detailed discussion in Volume 10 under verse 7 of Surah Ibrahim (page 278 onwards).

Ayahs

لَقَدْ كَانَ لِسَبَإٍ فِى مَسْكَنِهِمْ ءَايَةٌ ۖ جَنَّتَانِ عَن يَمِينٍ وَشِمَالٍ ۖ كُلُوا۟ مِن رِّزْقِ رَبِّكُمْ وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لَهُۥ ۚ بَلْدَةٌ طَيِّبَةٌ وَرَبٌّ غَفُورٌ (١٥)
There was certainly a sign for Sheba in their habitation: two gardens, to the right and to the left. ‘Eat of the provision of your Lord and give Him thanks: a good land and an all-forgiving Lord!’ (15)
فَأَعْرَضُوا۟ فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ سَيْلَ ٱلْعَرِمِ وَبَدَّلْنَـٰهُم بِجَنَّتَيْهِمْ جَنَّتَيْنِ ذَوَاتَىْ أُكُلٍ خَمْطٍ وَأَثْلٍ وَشَىْءٍ مِّن سِدْرٍ قَلِيلٍ (١٦)
But they disregarded [the path of Allah], so We unleashed upon them a violent flood and replaced their two gardens with two gardens bearing bitter fruit, tamarisk, and sparse lote trees. (16)
ذَٰلِكَ جَزَيْنَـٰهُم بِمَا كَفَرُوا۟ ۖ وَهَلْ نُجَـٰزِىٓ إِلَّا ٱلْكَفُورَ (١٧)
We requited them with that for their ingratitude. Do We not requite ingrates? (17)

Commentary

The Brilliant Civilization That Vanished Due to Ingratitude!

After mentioning the significant blessings that Allah bestowed upon David and Solomon, and how these two Prophets rose to fulfill the duty of gratitude, the discourse turns to another people who stood in stark contrast to them. They lived perhaps during the same era or shortly thereafter. They were a people to whom God granted various blessings, yet they chose the path of ingratitude. Consequently, Allah stripped them of His blessings, and they became so scattered and displaced that their life story became a lesson for the world; they were the "People of Saba" (Sheba).

The Holy Quran recounts their cautionary tale within five verses, touching upon important details and characteristics of their lives in this brief span:

First, It says: "For the people of Saba, there was a sign [of Divine power] in their dwelling place" (لَقَدْ كانَ لِسَبَإٍ فِي مَسْكَنِهِمْ آيَةٌ).

As we shall see, this great Divine sign originated from the fact that the people of Saba, utilizing specific geographical conditions, the formation of the surrounding mountains, and their God-given intelligence, managed to concentrate flash floods—which usually resulted in nothing but destruction—behind a powerful dam. Through this, they built a highly prosperous country. What greater sign is there than a factor of destruction being transformed into the primary factor of development?

There is debate among historians regarding who or what "Saba" (pronounced Saba’) refers to. However, the well-known view is that "Saba" is the name of the father of the Arabs of Yemen. According to a narration from the Holy Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, there was a man named "Saba" who had ten children, and from each of them, a tribe of the Arab tribes in that region emerged [^1]. Some have considered "Saba" to be the name of the land of Yemen or a region within it. The apparent meaning of the Holy Quran in the story of "Solomon" and the "Hoopoe" in Surah Al-Naml also indicates that "Saba" was a place name, where it says: وَ جِئْتُكَ مِنْ سَبَإٍ بِنَبَإٍ يَقِينٍ: "And I have come to you from Saba with certain news" (Naml: 22).

Meanwhile, the appearance of the verse under discussion suggests that Saba was a people living in that region, as the masculine plural pronoun (hum - "their") refers back to them.

However, there is no contradiction between these two interpretations, as it is possible that Saba was initially the name of a person, then all his descendants and tribe were called by that name, and subsequently, this name was transferred to their land as well.

Then, the Quran proceeds to describe this Divine sign that was at the disposal of the people of Saba, saying: "[There were] two gardens, on the right and on the left" (جَنَّتانِ عَنْ يَمِينٍ وَ شِمالٍ).

The story was that the people of Saba, by constructing a massive dam between the major mountains of that region, were able to store the abundant floodwaters—which would otherwise cause destruction or at least be wasted in the deserts—behind that great dam. By creating sluice gates in the dam, they brought the use of that massive reservoir under their control, thereby bringing vast and extensive lands under cultivation. The objection mentioned by Fakhr al-Razi here—that the existence of two gardens is not significant enough to be mentioned as a "sign"—and his subsequent answer to it, is in our view fundamentally unnecessary.
