This is because they were not just two simple, ordinary gardens; rather, they were a series of interconnected gardens on both sides of a great river irrigated by that massive dam. They were so blessed that—

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed.

—it is recorded in histories that if someone placed a basket on their head and passed under the trees during the fruit season, so much fruit would fall into it that the basket would be full after a short time! Is it not extraordinary that a flood, which is a cause of ruin, becomes such a cause of prosperity?

Does this not count as a great sign of God?

In addition to all this, an extraordinary security overshadowed that land, which itself was considered one of the signs of the Truth, as the Quran will point out later.

Then It adds: We told them: Eat from the provision of your Lord and be grateful to Him (كُلُوا مِنْ رِزْقِ رَبِّكُمْ وَ اشْكُرُوا لَهُ).

"A good land and a forgiving Lord" (بَلْدَةٌ طَيِّبَةٌ وَ رَبٌّ غَفُورٌ) [^1] [^2]. This short phrase reflects the collection of material and spiritual blessings in the most beautiful way. In terms of material blessings, they had a pure and wholesome land, free from various pollutions, from thieves and oppressors, from pests and calamities, from drought and famine, from insecurity and terror; it is even said it was free from stinging insects.

It had pure air and a refreshing breeze, fertile soil, and fruitful trees. As for spiritual blessings, Allah's forgiveness encompassed them; He would overlook their shortcomings and—

[^1]: Baldatun is the predicate of a hidden subject, estimated as: Hadhihi baldatun tayyibatun wa hadha Rabbun Ghafur (This is a good land and this is a forgiving Lord). [^2]: Whether this Divine message was conveyed through Prophets sent among them, as some commentators have said, or whether these conditions and circumstances conveyed such a message through the "language of state" and rational perception—both are possible.

—He would not subject them to punishment or afflict their land with calamity.

However, these ungrateful people did not appreciate the value of all these blessings. They did not emerge successful from the crucible of trial; they chose the path of ingratitude and turning away, and Allah punished them severely.

Therefore, in the next verse, He says: "But they turned away" (فَأَعْرَضُوا). They belittled Allah's blessings, took development and security for granted, became heedless of the remembrance of the Truth, and became intoxicated by wealth. The rich boasted over the poor and considered them a nuisance to their status, the details of which will come in the following verses.

It was here that the whip of punishment struck them, as the Quran says:

"So We sent upon them the flood of the Dam (Arim)" and their prosperous land was turned into a ruin (فَأَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ سَيْلَ الْعَرِمِ).

"Arim" originally comes from "aramah" (on the pattern of 'allamah), meaning harshness, ill-temper, and severity. Describing the flood with this word points to its extreme violence and destructiveness. The expression "Sayl al-'Arim" is, linguistically, the addition of a noun to its adjective.

Some have interpreted "Arim" to mean "field mice" which, by burrowing into the dam, caused its collapse (the issue of mice penetrating the dam is acceptable, as we will explain later, but the wording of the verse does not strongly align with this meaning).

In Lisan al-Arab, various meanings are given for the root "aram", including: an overwhelming flood, barriers built in valleys to harness water, and also a large field mouse [^1]. However, the most appropriate of all is the first meaning, which is also emphasized in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim.

Then the Quran describes the final state of this land: "And We exchanged their two gardens for two gardens of bitter fruit, tamarisk, and something of sparse lote trees" (وَ بَدَّلْناهُمْ بِجَنَّتَيْهِمْ جَنَّتَيْنِ ذَواتَيْ أُكُلٍ خَمْطٍ وَ أَثْلٍ وَ شَيْ‌ءٍ مِنْ سِدْرٍ قَلِيلٍ).

"Ukul" refers to any kind of edible substance. "Khamt" refers to a bitter plant. "Athl" refers to the "tamarisk" tree.

Thus, instead of all those lush and green trees, there remained a handful of wild, low-value desert trees, perhaps the most significant of which was the lote tree (sidr), which also existed among them in small quantities. (From this brief description, one can infer the full extent of what happened to them and their prosperous land).

It is possible that the mention of these three types of trees remaining in that ruined land refers to three different groups: some were harmful, some were useless, and some were of very little benefit.

In the next verse, as a conclusion, It explicitly states: "This We repaid them because they disbelieved" (ذلِكَ جَزَيْناهُمْ بِما كَفَرُوا).

But so that it is not imagined that this fate was exclusive to this group, rather—

[^1]: Lisan al-Arab, root 'aram.

—its universality for all who commit similar acts is certain, He adds: "And do We ever repay [in this way] except the ungrateful?" (وَ هَلْ نُجازِي إِلَّا الْكَفُورَ).

This was a summary of the story of the people of Saba, which will be discussed in more detail in the following verses.

Ayahs

وَجَعَلْنَا بَيْنَهُمْ وَبَيْنَ ٱلْقُرَى ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا قُرًى ظَـٰهِرَةً وَقَدَّرْنَا فِيهَا ٱلسَّيْرَ ۖ سِيرُوا۟ فِيهَا لَيَالِىَ وَأَيَّامًا ءَامِنِينَ (١٨)
We had placed between them and the towns which We had blessed hamlets prominent [from the main route], and We had ordained the course through them: ‘Travel through them in safety, night and day.’ (18)
فَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا بَـٰعِدْ بَيْنَ أَسْفَارِنَا وَظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ أَحَادِيثَ وَمَزَّقْنَـٰهُمْ كُلَّ مُمَزَّقٍ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ (١٩)
But they said, ‘Our Lord! Make the stages between our journeys far apart,’ and they wronged themselves. So We turned them into folktales and caused them to disintegrate totally. There are indeed signs in that for every patient and grateful [servant]. (19)

Commentary

We Disintegrated Them So Completely That They Became a Proverb!

In these verses, the discourse returns once more to the story of the people of Saba (Sheba), providing further details about them and describing their punishment more elaborately, in a way that serves as a very important and instructive lesson for every listener.

He says: We made their land so prosperous that We not only filled their cities with blessings but "We placed between them and the cities which We had blessed, visible cities and villages" (وَ جَعَلْنَا بَيْنَهُمْ وَ بَيْنَ الْقُرَى الَّتِي بَارَكْنَا فِيهَا قُرًى ظَاهِرَةً).

In fact, between them and the Blessed Land, there were continuous and linked settlements, and the distance between these villages was so small that they could see one another (and this is the meaning of "قُرًى ظَاهِرَةً"—visible villages).

Some commentators have interpreted "visible villages" differently, stating: It refers to villages that were situated directly and conspicuously along the path, where travelers could easily stop, or that these villages were located on heights and were apparent and visible to passersby.

As for which region is meant by the "Blessed Lands," most commentators have interpreted it as the land of Greater Syria (Syria, Palestine, and Jordan), as this expression is used for that region in the first verse of Surah Al-Isra and verse 81 of Surah Al-Anbiya.

However, some commentators have suggested it might refer to the settlements of "Sana'a" or "Ma'rib," both located in the Yemen region. This interpretation is not unlikely, because the distance between "Yemen," at the southernmost point of the Arabian Peninsula, and "Greater Syria," at the northernmost point, is so vast and covered by dry, scorching deserts that interpreting the verse as such seems very remote and is not recorded in histories. Some have also suggested it refers to the land of "Mecca," which is also unlikely.

This was regarding prosperity; but since development alone is not enough, and its most vital and fundamental condition is "security," He adds: "And We determined the stages of journey between them" (وَ قَدَّرْنَا فِيهَا السَّيْرَ).

And We said to them: "Travel through them by night and day in complete security" (سِيرُوا فِيهَا لَيَالِيَ وَ أَيَّاماً آمِنِينَ).

In this way, the villages had appropriate and calculated distances, and they were in ultimate security regarding attacks by wild beasts and desert predators, or thieves and highwaymen, such that people could continue their journey without provisions or a mount, without needing to travel in groups or use armed guards, and without any fear or dread regarding the insecurity of the road or lack of water and food.

Regarding who conveyed the command "Travel through them..." (سِيرُوا فِيهَا...) to them, there are two possibilities: one is that it was conveyed to them by their prophets, and the other is that the very state of that prosperous land and those safe and peaceful roads spoke this language.

The mention of "nights" (لَيَالِيَ) before "days" (أَيَّاماً) might be because the importance of security lies in the nights—both security from highway robbers and desert beasts—otherwise, ensuring security during the day is easier.

However, these ungrateful people, in the face of all those great divine blessings that encompassed their entire lives—like many other affluent nations—fell victim to pride and negligence. The intoxication of wealth and their lack of capacity led them to choose the path of ingratitude, deviate from the path of truth, and become indifferent to divine commands.

Among their insane requests was that they asked Allah to create distances between their journeys: "They said: Our Lord! Lengthen the distance between our journeys" (فَقَالُوا رَبَّنَا بَاعِدْ بَيْنَ أَسْفَارِنَا).

Their intention was for distances to be created between these prosperous villages and for dry deserts to appear, because the rich and wealthy did not want the low-income people to travel side-by-side with them, going wherever they wished without provisions or a mount! It was as if travel was one of their points of pride and a sign of power and wealth, and this privilege and superiority had to be permanently recorded for them! Or perhaps their ease and welfare had made them restless, just as the Children of Israel grew tired of "Manna" and "Salwa" (two heavenly foods) and asked God for onions, garlic, and lentils! Some have also suggested that the phrase "lengthen the distance between our journeys" indicates they became so comfort-seeking that they were no longer willing to travel for the use of pastures for livestock or for trade and agriculture, and they asked God to let them remain always in their homeland and for the time intervals between their journeys to increase! But the first interpretation seems best of all.

In any case, "they wronged themselves by this action of theirs" (وَ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ).

Yes, if they thought they were wronging others, they were mistaken; they had taken a dagger and were piercing their own chests, and the smoke of all these fires went into their own eyes.

What a fascinating expression! Following this sentence about their painful fate, the Quran says: We punished them so, and folded up their lives such that "We made them stories and tales for others" (فَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ أَحَادِيثَ).

Yes, from all that flourishing life and brilliant, extensive civilization, nothing remained but reports on people's tongues, a memory in minds, and lines on the pages of history, "and We tore them into utter pieces" (وَ مَزَّقْنَاهُمْ كُلَّ مُمَزَّقٍ).

Their land became so ruined that the ability to stay was taken from them, and to continue living, every group was forced to turn in a different direction. Like autumn leaves caught in a violent wind, each was thrown to a corner, such that their dispersion became a "proverb." Whenever people wanted to say a certain population was severely disintegrated, they would say "They scattered like the hands of Saba!" (تفرقوا ايادى سبا). [^1] According to some commentators, the tribe of "Ghassan" went to Syria, "Asad" to Oman, "Khuza'ah" toward Tihamah, and the clan of "Anmar" to Yathrib. [^2] At the end of the verse, He says: "Indeed in that are signs for everyone who is patient and grateful" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَاتٍ لِكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ).

Why is it that the "patient" and "grateful" can take a lesson from these events? (Especially considering that Sabbar and Shakur are both intensive forms, expressing repetition and emphasis).

This is because, through their patience and steadfastness, they rein in the rebellious mount of whims and desires and are powerful against sins; and through their gratitude, they are ready and vigilant on the path of obedience to Allah. For this reason, they take lessons well. But those who ride the mount of whims and are indifferent to divine favors, how can they take lessons from these events?!

Key Points

1- The Extraordinary Story of the People of Saba

As gathered from the Quran, Islamic narrations, and history, they were a population living in the south of the Arabian Peninsula, possessing a sublime government and a brilliant civilization.

The soil of Yemen was extensive and fertile, but despite this readiness, it was not utilized because it had no major river. Torrential rains fell in the mountains, and their waters went to waste in the plains. The intelligent people of this land thought of utilizing these waters and built many dams at sensitive points, the most important and water-rich of which was the "Ma'rib" Dam.

Ma'rib was a city located at the end of one of these valleys, and the massive floods from the "Sarat" mountains passed by it. At the mouth of this valley and the foot of the two "Balaq" mountains, they built a massive and powerful dam and created various water channels within it. The water storage behind the dam became so great that they were able to create very beautiful gardens and blessed fields on both sides of the river path leading to the dam.

As we mentioned, the prosperous villages of this land were almost connected, and the wide shadows of the trees joined hands. So much fruit appeared on their branches that it is said if someone placed a basket on their head and passed under them, fruits would fall into it one after another and it would be filled in a short time.

The abundance of blessings mixed with security had prepared a very comfortable environment for a pure life—an environment ready for obedience to the Lord and perfection in spiritual aspects.

But they did not appreciate all these blessings; they consigned Allah to oblivion and engaged in ingratitude, boasting, and fueling class distinctions.

In some histories, it is mentioned that desert rats, away from the eyes of the proud and intoxicated people, turned to the walls of this earthen dam and weakened it from within. Suddenly, a heavy rain fell and a massive flood moved. The dam walls, unable to withstand the pressure of the flood, suddenly collapsed, and the vast amount of water accumulated behind the dam suddenly poured out, destroying all the settlements, gardens, fields, crops, and livestock, and instantly ruining the palaces and magnificent, beautiful houses. It turned that prosperous land into a dry, barren desert, and from all those lush gardens and fruitful trees, only a few bitter "Arak" (Salvadora persica), "Tamarix," and a few "Sidr" (Lote) trees remained. The singing birds migrated from there, and owls and crows took their place. [^1] Yes, when Allah wishes to manifest His power, He blows away a great civilization with a few rats! So that servants may become acquainted with their own weakness and not become proud in times of power.

[^1]: This proverb is narrated in two forms: "تفرقوا ايدى سبا" and "ايادى سبا". In the first case, it refers to the scattering of their army and individuals, and in the second case, it refers to the scattering of their wealth, facilities, and gifts, because "ayadi" is usually used to mean blessings.

[^2]: "Tafsir al-Qurtubi" and "Tafsir Abu al-Futuh al-Razi" under the verses in question.

[^1]: Adapted from "Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan," "Qisas al-Quran," and other commentaries.

2- A Historical Miracle of the Quran

The Holy Quran has brought the story of the "People of Saba" in the above verses, and for a long time, world historians expressed ignorance regarding the existence of such a people and such a civilization.

Interestingly, before modern discoveries, historians did not mention the dynasty of the kings of Saba and their great civilization, and they considered "Saba" only a hypothetical person who was the father of the founder of the "Himyar" state. Meanwhile, in the Quran, there is a Surah named after this people, and it refers to one of the manifestations of their civilization, which is the construction of the historic Ma'rib Dam. However, after the discovery of the historical remains of this people in Yemen, the opinion of scholars changed.

The reason why the remains of the Sabaean civilization were not excavated until recently was twofold: One was the difficulty of the path and the extreme heat, and the other was the suspicion of the inhabitants of these areas toward foreigners, which uninformed Europeans sometimes interpreted as savagery. This continued until a few archaeologists, due to their intense interest in discovering the secrets of the Sabaean remains, were able to enter the heart of the city of "Ma'rib" and its surroundings. They took samples of the numerous remains, scripts, and carvings recorded on stones. Thereafter, groups followed one after another in the 19th century AD, bringing valuable artifacts from there to Europe. From the collection of these carvings, scripts, and other remains, which reached a thousand pieces, they deduced the details of this people's civilization and even the date of the construction of the Ma'rib Dam and other characteristics. It was proven to Westerners that what the Quran stated in this regard is not a legend, but a historical reality of which they were unaware, such that they have now been able to draw maps of this massive dam, the water passage, the channels of the orchards on the left and right, and its other features. [^1]

[^1]: Farhang-e Qisas-e Quran, entry "Saba" (summarized).

3- Important Points of Lesson in a Short Story

The placement of the story of the "People of Saba" after the account of "Solomon" in the Holy Quran carries a specific meaning.

  1. David and Solomon were great prophets who established a massive government and created a brilliant civilization, but with the death of David and Solomon, this civilization began to decline. The people of Saba also established a great civilization that disintegrated with the breaking of the "Ma'rib" Dam. Interestingly, according to narrations, the staff of Solomon (عليه السلام) was eaten by a termite, and the massive Ma'rib Dam was pierced by a desert rat, so that this proud human may know that material favors, however great and dazzling, can sometimes collapse with a breeze and be overturned by an insect or a small animal. This is so that the aware do not set their hearts on it, the believers do not become enslaved by it, and the proud awaken from the intoxication of pride and do not choose the path of arrogance and oppression.

  2. Beyond this, two faces of magnificent civilization are seen here: one was divine (Rahmani) and the other eventually became satanic, but neither this nor that remained! Both faced annihilation.

  3. This point is also noteworthy: the proud among the people of Saba, who could not bear to see the masses of people alongside them and imagined there should be a great dam and a massive border between the aristocratic minority and the low-income majority so they would never mix, asked Allah for the distancing of settlements and the lengthening of travel. Allah answered this prayer of theirs, and they were so disintegrated that every group went in a different direction, and they became so distant from one another that if they wanted to find each other, they would have to be traveling for a lifetime!

  4. Whenever someone looked at the state of that land before the onslaught of the "Flood of 'Arim" and after it, they would not believe that this is the same land that was once full of lush, green, and fruitful trees, which today appears in the form of a terrifying desert where individual Tamarix, Arak, and Sidr trees are seen like travelers who have lost their way and been scattered. This scene speaks through its state: the land of human existence is also like this. If his creative forces are harnessed and his talents are used correctly, it brings forth vibrant gardens of knowledge, action, and moral virtues. But if the dam of piety (Taqwa) breaks, and instincts cover the land of human life in the form of a destructive flood, nothing but a worthless ruin will remain. Sometimes a seemingly small factor gradually strikes the root and collapses everything; one must fear and beware of even these small matters.

  5. The final word we deem necessary to mention here is that this extraordinary event once again proves the truth that the death of a human is hidden within the heart of his life, and the very thing that is one day the source of his life and prosperity may another day become the cause of death and destruction.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ صَدَّقَ عَلَيْهِمْ إِبْلِيسُ ظَنَّهُۥ فَٱتَّبَعُوهُ إِلَّا فَرِيقًا مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٢٠)
Certainly Iblis had his conjecture come true about them. So they followed him—all except a part of the faithful. (20)
وَمَا كَانَ لَهُۥ عَلَيْهِم مِّن سُلْطَـٰنٍ إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِمَّنْ هُوَ مِنْهَا فِى شَكٍّ ۗ وَرَبُّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَفِيظٌ (٢١)
He had no authority over them, but that We may ascertain those who believe in the Hereafter from those who are in doubt about it, and your Lord is watchful over all things. (21)

Commentary

No One is Forced to Follow Satan's Whispers!

These verses are, in fact, a general conclusion drawn from the story of the "People of Saba" mentioned in the preceding verses. We saw how they fell into such misery and failure due to their surrender to carnal desires and the whispers of Iblis.

In the first verse, He says: "And indeed, Iblis found his assumption about them (and any group that follows Iblis) to be true" (وَ لَقَدْ صَدَّقَ عَلَيْهِمْ إِبْلِيسُ ظَنَّهُ‌).

"So they followed him, except for a party of the believers" (فَاتَّبَعُوهُ إِلَّا فَرِيقاً مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

In other words, the prediction of Iblis—which he made after refusing to prostrate to Adam and being expelled from the Divine Presence, saying: فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ‌: "By Your might, I will surely mislead them all, except Your chosen servants among them"—turned out to be correct regarding this group ^1. Although he spoke these words based on assumption and estimation, this very assumption eventually became a reality. These weak-willed individuals with fragile faith followed him in groups. Only a small party of believers remained who shattered the chains of Satan's whispers and were not deceived by his traps; they came free, lived free, and departed free. Although they were few in number, in terms of value, each was equal to a world:

"They are the fewest in number, but the greatest in rank before Allah." [^2]

In the following verse, regarding the whispers of Iblis and those who fall under his sphere of influence versus those who remain outside it, He points to two matters: First, He says: "And he had no authority over them" and he does not force anyone to follow him (وَ ما كانَ لَهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ‌).

It is we who grant him permission to enter and issue his transit permit through the borders of the country of the body into our hearts! This is the same point that the Quran quotes elsewhere from the words of Satan himself:

وَ ما كانَ لِي عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ إِلَّا أَنْ دَعَوْتُكُمْ فَاسْتَجَبْتُمْ لِي‌: "I had no authority over you except that I invited you, and you responded to me" (Ibrahim-22).

However, it is clear that after his invitation is accepted by faithless and lust-driven individuals, he does not sit idly by; rather, he solidifies the foundations of his authority over their existence.

Therefore, in the continuation of the verse, He adds: "The purpose of allowing Iblis freedom in his whisperings was so that those who believe in the Hereafter might be distinguished from those who are in doubt about it" (إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِالْآخِرَةِ مِمَّنْ هُوَ مِنْها فِي شَكٍ‌) ^1. It is obvious that Allah has been aware of everything that will happen in this world until eternity since pre-eternity. Therefore, the phrase "لِنَعْلَمَ" (so that We may know) does not mean that We do not know the believers in the Hereafter from those in doubt and that Satan's whispers must intervene for them to be known. Rather, the meaning of this phrase is the external realization of Allah's knowledge. This is because Allah never punishes anyone based solely on His knowledge of their inner state or their potential actions; rather, a field of trial must be provided, and the whispers of devils and carnal desires must begin so that everyone may manifest what is within them with complete freedom of will and choice, and thus Allah's knowledge attains external realization. For as long as no action is performed externally, the desert of reward or punishment is not established.

In other words: until that which is potential becomes actual, no one is rewarded or punished based solely on the goodness or wickedness of their inner self.

At the end of the verse, as a warning to all servants, He says: "And your Lord is Guardian over all things" (وَ رَبُّكَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ حَفِيظٌ).

This is so the followers of Satan do not imagine that any of their deeds or words will vanish in this world or that Allah will forget them. No, never; rather, Allah preserves and guards everything for the Day of Recompense.

[^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings. ^1: Based on the meaning we explained in the commentary of the verse, the exception here is a "connected exception" (istithna' muttasil), supported by what appears in Surah Al-Hijr, verse 42: إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ‌, because the appearance of this verse is that Satan does gain authority over the deviators—though some commentators have also considered the possibility of it being a "disconnected exception" (istithna' munqati').

Ayahs

قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا لَهُمْ فِيهِمَا مِن شِرْكٍ وَمَا لَهُۥ مِنْهُم مِّن ظَهِيرٍ (٢٢)
Say, ‘Invoke those whom you claim [to be gods] besides Allah! They do not control [even] an atom’s weight in the heavens or the earth, nor do they have any share in [either of] them, nor is any of them His helper.’ (22)
وَلَا تَنفَعُ ٱلشَّفَـٰعَةُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا لِمَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُۥ ۚ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا فُزِّعَ عَن قُلُوبِهِمْ قَالُوا۟ مَاذَا قَالَ رَبُّكُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ ٱلْحَقَّ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْكَبِيرُ (٢٣)
Intercession is of no avail with Him, except for those whom He permits. When fear is lifted from their hearts, they say, ‘What did your Lord say?’ They say, ‘The truth, and He is the All-exalted, the All-great.’ (23)
۞ قُلْ مَن يَرْزُقُكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَإِنَّآ أَوْ إِيَّاكُمْ لَعَلَىٰ هُدًى أَوْ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٢٤)
Say, ‘Who provides for you from the heavens and the earth?’ Say, ‘Allah! Indeed either we or you are rightly guided or in manifest error.’ (24)
قُل لَّا تُسْـَٔلُونَ عَمَّآ أَجْرَمْنَا وَلَا نُسْـَٔلُ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٢٥)
Say, ‘You will not be questioned about our guilt, nor shall we be questioned about what you do.’ (25)
قُلْ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنَا رَبُّنَا ثُمَّ يَفْتَحُ بَيْنَنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَهُوَ ٱلْفَتَّاحُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٢٦)
Say, ‘Our Lord will bring us together, then He will judge between us with justice, and He is the All-knowing Judge.’ (26)
قُلْ أَرُونِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَلْحَقْتُم بِهِۦ شُرَكَآءَ ۖ كَلَّا ۚ بَلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٢٧)
Say, ‘Show me those whom you associate with Him as partners.’ No! [They can never show any such partner]. Indeed, He is Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise. (27)

Commentary

Tell Me Why...

At the beginning of this Surah, we mentioned that a significant portion of its verses discusses the Origin (Creation), the Resurrection, and righteous beliefs, and from their connection, a collection of true gnosis is obtained.

In this section of verses, the polytheists are effectively brought to trial. With the striking blows of logical questions, they are brought to their knees, and the baselessness of their decayed logic regarding the intercession of idols is exposed.

In this series of verses, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is addressed five times with the command: "Say to them..." and each time, a new point regarding the fate of idols and idol-worship is raised, such that by the end, one clearly feels that no school of thought is hollower than that of the idolaters—in fact, it cannot even be called a school or a religion.

In the first verse, He says: "Say to them: Call upon those whom you imagine (to be gods) besides Allah [1], but know that they will never answer your prayers nor untie any knot in your affairs" (قُلِ ادْعُوا الَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ). Then, providing the reason for this statement, He says: "This is because these fabricated deities neither possess an atom's weight in the heavens or the earth, nor do they have any partnership or share in their creation and ownership, nor has any of them been a helper to Allah in creation!" (لا يَمْلِكُونَ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ لا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ ما لَهُمْ فِيهِما مِنْ شِرْكٍ وَ ما لَهُ مِنْهُمْ مِنْ ظَهِيرٍ).

If they were capable of solving a problem, they would have to possess one of these three attributes: either independent ownership of something in the heavens and the earth, or at least partnership with Allah in the matter of creation, or finally, assisting the Lord in any of these affairs.

While it is clear that the Necessary Being (Wajib al-Wujud) is One, and all others are contingent beings (Mumkin al-Wujud) dependent upon Him; if His grace were withdrawn from them for a single moment, they would head toward the realm of non-existence: "If He shows a sign of disdain for a moment, all forms shall collapse!"

It is interesting that He says: مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ لا فِي الْأَرْضِ (an atom's weight in the heavens or the earth); meaning, beings that do not own anything even to the weight of an insignificant atom in these vast heavens and wide earth—what problem of their own can they solve, let alone yours?!

[1] In this sentence, there are actually two implied parts: first, after "زعمتم" (you claimed), the phrase "that they are gods" is implied; and after "مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ" (besides Allah), the phrase "they do not respond to your prayer" is implied. Altogether it becomes: "Say: Call upon those whom you claimed are gods besides Allah; they do not respond to you."

Here, a question immediately comes to mind: if this is the case, then what becomes of the "intercession" of the intercessors? In the next verse, He addresses this question, saying: If there are intercessors in the presence of Allah, it is only by His permission and command, for: "No intercession avails with Him except for those whom He has permitted" (وَ لا تَنْفَعُ الشَّفاعَةُ عِنْدَهُ إِلَّا لِمَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ).

Therefore, the excuse of the idolaters for worshipping idols—when they said: "هؤُلاءِ شُفَعاؤُنا عِنْدَ اللَّهِ" (These are our intercessors with Allah! - Yunus: 18)—is hereby cut off, because Allah has never granted them any permission for intercession.

Regarding whether the phrase "إِلَّا لِمَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ" (except for him whom He permits) refers to the intercessors or those being interceded for, commentators have offered two possibilities. However, since the previous verse discussed idols and they imagined idols to be their intercessors, it is more appropriate that it refers to the "intercessors."

Is the "intercession" intended here for this world or the hereafter? Both are possible, but the subsequent phrases indicate that the focus is on the intercession of the hereafter.

Thus, after this phrase, it is said: On that day, anxiety and terror will overwhelm the hearts (both the intercessors and those seeking intercession will be drowned in anxiety, waiting to see to whom Allah will grant permission for intercession, and for whom? This state of anxiety and concern continues) "until when fear is removed from their hearts, and the command is issued from Allah's side" (حَتَّى إِذا فُزِّعَ عَنْ قُلُوبِهِمْ) [1]. In any case, there is a great commotion on that day; the eyes of those seeking intercession are fixed upon the intercessors, and through the language of their state or their speech, they imploringly request intercession from them.

But the intercessors also have their eyes fixed on Allah's command, to see how and for whom He will grant permission for intercession. This general and universal terror and anxiety continue until the command for intercession is issued by the Wise Allah for those who are worthy of it.

It is here that both groups turn to one another and ask (or the criminals ask the intercessors): "What did your Lord command?" (قالُوا ما ذا قالَ رَبُّكُمْ).

"They say in response: Allah spoke the truth" (قالُوا الْحَقَّ).

And the truth is nothing other than the permission for intercession for those who have not completely severed their connection with Allah's threshold—not for the polluted ones who broke all links of connection and became completely estranged from Allah, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and His friends.

And at the end of the verse, He adds: "He is the Most High, the Most Great" (وَ هُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْكَبِيرُ).

This phrase is a continuation of the intercessors' speech and its complement; in fact, they are saying:

[1] "فزع" (fear) from the root "فزع", whenever it is made transitive by "عن", means the removal of fear and the dispelling of terror and anxiety. This root carries this meaning even in its simple triliteral form when made transitive with "عن".

Since Allah is the Most High and the Most Great, whatever He commands is the very reality, and every reality conforms to His command.

What we have stated above is the closest interpretation that is harmonious and consistent with the phrases of the verse. Here, commentators have mentioned other interpretations as well, and surprisingly, in some of them, the connection between the beginning and end of the verse, and what precedes and follows it, has not been considered at all.

In the next verse, He approaches the invalidation of the polytheists' beliefs through another path, raising the issue of "Provision" (Razqiyyah) after the issue of "Creation" (Khaliqiyyah) which was discussed in the previous verses. This proof is also in the form of a question and answer to awaken their dormant consciences through this method, so that they may realize their mistake from the answer that wells up from within them.

He says: "Say: Who provides for you from the heavens and the earth and places its blessings at your disposal?" (قُلْ مَنْ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ). It is obvious that none of them could say that these stone and wooden idols send down rain from the sky, grow plants from the earth, and place terrestrial and celestial resources at our disposal.

Interestingly, without waiting for their response, He immediately says:

"Say: Allah" (قُلِ اللَّهُ).

Say: It is Allah who is the source of all these blessings; meaning the matter is so clear and obvious that it does not require a response from the opponent. Rather, the questioner and the listener are in unison, because even the polytheists considered Allah to be the Creator and the Giver of provisions, and they only attributed the status of intercession to the idols.

It is also noteworthy that the provisions of the Lord that reach humans from the direction of the sky are not limited to rain; "the light and heat of the sun" and the "air" present in the earth's atmosphere are even more important than the life-giving drops of rain.

Just as the blessings of the earth are not limited to plants, but rather all types of underground water sources and various minerals—some of which were discovered at that time and some which were revealed over time—are all included under this title.

At the end of the verse, He points to a matter that can itself form the basis of a proof—a realistic proof coupled with the utmost fairness and politeness, such that the opponent descends from the mount of stubbornness and pride and begins to reflect and think. He says: "Surely, either we or you are upon guidance or in manifest error!" (وَ إِنَّا أَوْ إِيَّاكُمْ لَعَلى‌ هُدىً أَوْ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ) [1]. This points to the fact that our belief and yours are in clear contradiction; therefore, it is impossible for both to be right, since the union of two contradictories or opposites is impossible. Thus, certainly one group is the people of guidance and the second group is caught in error.

Now reflect: which one is guided and which one is lost? Look at the signs in both groups: with which group are the signs of guidance, and with the other, the signs of error?!

And this is one of the best methods of debate and discussion—to prompt the opponent to think and self-reflect. The idea that some have considered this a type of Taqiyyah (dissimulation) is a grave mistake.

[1] This sentence is implied to return to two sentences in this order: "And surely we are either upon guidance or in manifest error, and surely you are either upon guidance or in manifest error" (Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 7, p. 388).

It is interesting that He mentions "guidance" with the word "على" (upon) and "error" with "في" (in); indicating that the guided are, as it were, seated upon a swift mount or positioned atop a height, completely in control of everything, while the lost are submerged in their error and the darkness of their ignorance.

It is also noteworthy that He first spoke of "guidance" and then of "error," because at the start of the first phrase He says "we" and then says "you," as a subtle and faint hint toward the guidance of the first group and the lack of guidance of the second!

Although a group of commentators considers the description "مبين" (manifest) to relate only to "ضلال" (error), because there are types of error, and the error of polytheism is the most manifest of all.

However, the possibility also exists that this description applies to both "guidance" and "error," because in such cases in the words of the eloquent, the description is not repeated. Therefore, both "guidance" is described as "manifest" and "error" as well, just as in other verses of the Quran this description is seen in both parts [1].

The next verse continues the same argument in another form—again with that same fair tone that brings the adversary down from the mount of stubbornness and pride—saying:

"Say: You will not be questioned about our sins, nor shall we be questioned about what you do" (قُلْ لا تُسْئَلُونَ عَمَّا أَجْرَمْنا وَ لا نُسْئَلُ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ).

Surprisingly, here the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is commanded to use the term "جرم" (crime/sin) regarding himself, and regarding the opponents, the term for the "deeds" they perform! In this way, he clarifies the reality that everyone must be accountable for their own actions and conduct, because the results of every human's actions—whether ugly or beautiful—reach themselves.

Additionally, it contains a subtle hint that if we insist on guiding you, it is not because your sin will be written against us or that your polytheism harms us; we insist on this out of compassion, truth-seeking, and the pursuit of right.

The next verse is, in fact, the statement of the result of the two previous verses. For when He warned them that one of our two groups is on the truth and the other on falsehood, and also warned that each of us is responsible for our own actions, He proceeds to state the reality of how everyone's situation will be addressed, how truth and falsehood will be separated, and how each will receive reward or punishment according to their responsibilities. He says: "Say to them: Our Lord will gather us all on the Day of Resurrection, then He will judge between us with truth" and separate us from one another, so that the guided may be distinguished from the lost, and each may reach the result of their actions (قُلْ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنا رَبُّنا ثُمَّ يَفْتَحُ بَيْنَنا بِالْحَقِّ).

If you see today that everyone is mixed together and everyone claims "I am on the truth and I am the one saved," this situation will not continue forever. The day of the separation of ranks will eventually arrive, because the "Lordship" (Rububiyyah) of the Lord necessitates that the "genuine" be eventually separated from the "counterfeit," the "pure" from the "impure," and the "truth" from the "falsehood," and each be placed in its own course.

Now think: what will you do on that day? And in which rank will you be placed? And have you prepared an answer for the Lord's questions on that day? At the end of the verse, to clarify that this task is certainly doable, He adds:

"He is the Judge, the All-Knowing Separator" (وَ هُوَ الْفَتَّاحُ الْعَلِيمُ).

These two names, which are among the Beautiful Names of Allah (Asma al-Husna), one refers to His power over the matter of separating the ranks, and the other to His infinite knowledge, because separating the ranks of truth and falsehood from one another is impossible without these two.

Relying on the title "رب" (Lord) in the above verse points to the fact that Allah is the Owner and Nurturer of us all, and this status requires that He provide the program for such a day; in fact, it is a subtle hint toward one of the proofs for the "Resurrection."

The word "فتح" (opening/victory), as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, originally means removing complexity and difficulty. This is of two kinds: sometimes it is seen with the eye, like opening a lock, and sometimes it is perceived with the intellect, like opening the complexity of sorrows and griefs, or opening the secrets of sciences, and likewise judging between two people and opening the problem of their dispute and litigation.

Therefore, if this word is used regarding the separation of ranks, especially where everyone is mixed together, it is for this reason; because in addition to separation, judgment and arbitration—which is one of the meanings of fath—also take place among them, and He compensates each according to what they deserve.

It is noteworthy that in some narrations, emphasis is placed on reciting "يا فتاح" (O Opener) for solving problems, because this great Name of Allah, which comes in the intensive form of "فتح", expresses the power of the Lord to open every difficulty, remove every sorrow and grief, and provide the means for every opening and victory. In reality, no one is the "Opener" (Fattah) but Him, and the "Key" (Miftah) to all closed doors is in the hand of His power.

In the last verse under discussion, which is the fifth command to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), He returns once more to the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) from which the discussion began, and concludes the discussion with this issue:

He says: "Say: Show me those whom you have joined with Him as partners" (قُلْ أَرُونِيَ الَّذِينَ أَلْحَقْتُمْ بِهِ شُرَكاءَ).

What value or merit do they have? If you mean this handful of lifeless and silent stones and wood, what misery and shame that you take the fabrications of your own hands from the world of minerals—which are the lowest of beings—and imagine them equal to the Great Allah.

And if you consider these as symbols of spirits and angels, it is still a calamity and error, because they too are His creatures and submissive to His command.

Therefore, following this phrase, He draws a line of cancellation through all these delusions with a single word, saying: "Nay, never is it so!" (كلا).

These never have the value of being deities, and there is nothing of reality in these imaginations of yours. Enough! Wake up! How long will you continue this incorrect path?! In reality, "كلا" is a small word that encompasses all these meanings.

And finally, to emphasize and consolidate this speech, He says: "Nay, He alone is Allah, the Almighty, the Wise" (بَلْ هُوَ اللَّهُ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ).

His Might and invincibility necessitate that no one finds a way into the sanctuary of His Lordship, and His Wisdom necessitates that He applies this power appropriately.

Yes, possessing these attributes is a sign of His being the Necessary Being (Wajib al-Wujud), and the Necessary Being is infinite existence; He can never be multiple and has no partner or peer, because any multiplicity would make Him limited and contingent, whereas infinite existence is always one (reflect carefully).

Key Point: The Way to Conquer Hearts!

It has often been seen that virtuous and learned individuals, due to lack of familiarity with the techniques of discussion and argument and failure to observe psychological aspects, can never influence the thoughts of others.

Conversely, we know of individuals who are not at that level scientifically but are victorious and successful in attracting hearts, conquering souls, and influencing the thoughts of others.

The main reason is that the manner of presenting discussions and the way of interacting with the opposing party must be coupled with principles of ethics and psychology so as not to trigger negative aspects in the opponent and force them into stubbornness and enmity. Rather, on the contrary, it should awaken their conscience and revive the spirit of truth-seeking within them.

The important thing here is to know that a human is not merely thought and intellect to submit only to the power of logic; rather, in addition to that, a collection of various "emotions" and "feelings"—which form an important part of their soul—is hidden within them, which must be satisfied in a correct and reasonable manner.

The Quran has taught us this way and method: how to present logical discussions to opponents while blending them with ethical principles so that they penetrate the depths of their souls.

The condition for influence is that the opposing party feels the speaker possesses the following qualities:

  1. He believes in his own words, and what he says arises from the depths of his soul.
  2. His goal in discussion is truth-seeking, not seeking superiority or dominance.
  3. He never wants to humiliate the opponent or aggrandize himself.
  4. What he says, he says out of compassion, and he has no personal or private interests in this work.
  5. He respects the opposing party and for this reason, does not forget politeness in discussion in his expressions.
  6. He does not want to needlessly provoke the opponent's sense of stubbornness, and if a subject has been discussed sufficiently, he contents himself with that and avoids insisting on the discussion or forcing his word.
  7. He is fair and never loses the side of fairness, even if the opposing party does not observe these principles.
  8. He does not want to impose his thoughts on others, but rather desires to create an inner spark in others so that, while remaining free, this self-sparking leads them to the truth.

Careful reflection on the above verses—the way the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) interacted with opponents by Allah's command, which was coupled with interesting subtleties—is a very vivid witness to the discussions above.

He sometimes goes so far as to not even precisely determine that "we are on the path of guidance and you are on the path of error," but rather says: "either we or you are on the path of guidance or in error," so that they may fall into thought: in which group are the signs of guidance and error?

Or he says: "On the Day of Resurrection, Allah will judge between all of us and compensate everyone according to what they deserve."

Of course, it cannot be denied that all of this applies to those for whom there is hope of guidance; otherwise, with stubborn, oppressive, and ruthless enemies for whom there is no hope of acceptance, the Quran interacts differently [1]. An analysis of the ways the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Imams (علیهم السلام) discussed with their opponents is a very vivid model for this topic. As an example, pay attention to what is recorded in the books of Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) in this regard:

In the introduction to the famous Hadith of Tawhid of "Mufaddal ibn Umar," we read: He says, "I was by the grave of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) thinking about the greatness of the Prophet's status, when suddenly I saw 'Ibn Abi al-Awja' (a famous materialist) enter and sit in a corner such that I could hear his speech. When his friends gathered around him, he began speaking blasphemous words, the result of which was the denial of the prophethood of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) and, higher than that, the denial of Allah, Blessed and Exalted. He expressed this very mischievously and calculatedly.

I became severely angry and upset upon hearing his words. I stood up and shouted: 'O enemy of Allah! Have you taken the path of atheism? And have you denied the God who created you in the best of forms?'...

'Ibn Abi al-Awja' turned to me and said: 'Who are you? If you are from the scholars of theology (Kalam), bring proof so that we may follow you; and if you are not, do not speak. And if you are from the followers of Ja'far ibn Muhammad al-Sadiq, he does not speak to us like this, nor does he interact as you do.'

He has heard more than this from us and has never cursed or insulted us, nor has he followed the path of violence or transgression in responding to us. He is a forbearing, wise, intelligent, and dignified man, who is never overtaken by levity. He listens well to our words, hears our arguments, and becomes aware of our proofs. When we have said everything and imagined that we have triumphed over him, he begins to speak with dignity. With short sentences and condensed words, he answers all our proofs and cuts off our excuses, such that we have no power to respond. If you are from his companions, speak to us like this." [1]

[1] In Volume 16 of Tafsir Nemouneh, under verse 46 of Surah al-Ankabut, we also had a detailed discussion in this regard. [1] Tawhid al-Mufaddal (beginning of the book).

Ayahs

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا كَآفَّةً لِّلنَّاسِ بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٢٨)
We did not send you except as a bearer of good news and warner to all mankind, but most people do not know. (28)
وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٢٩)
They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’ (29)
قُل لَّكُم مِّيعَادُ يَوْمٍ لَّا تَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ عَنْهُ سَاعَةً وَلَا تَسْتَقْدِمُونَ (٣٠)
Say, ‘Your promised hour is a day that you shall neither defer nor advance by an hour.’ (30)

Commentary

You Are Commissioned for All the Worlds!

The first verse under discussion speaks of the Prophethood of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and the subsequent verses discuss the Resurrection. Given that the preceding verses also spoke of Monotheism (Tawhid), they form a complete set of religious beliefs suitable for Meccan Surahs like Surah Saba.

First, referring to the vastness of the Prophet's invitation and the universality of his Prophethood to all human beings, it says: "And We have not sent you except to all mankind as a bringer of glad tidings and a warner, but most of the people do not know" (وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَاكَ إِلَّا كَافَّةً لِلنَّاسِ بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا وَلَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ).

"Kaffah" (كَافَّةً) is derived from the root "kaff" (كف), meaning the palm of the hand. Since a human uses their hand to grasp objects or push them away, this word has sometimes been used to mean "gathering" and sometimes "preventing."

In the verse under discussion, commentators have considered both meanings possible. First, that it means "gathering," in which case the concept of the verse is what we mentioned above: "We did not send you except for the entirety of the world's people," explaining the global nature of the invitation of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله.

Numerous narrations cited in the commentary of this verse through both Shia and Sunni channels also reinforce this interpretation.

Therefore, the content of the verse is like verse 1 of Surah Al-Furqan, which says: تَبَارَكَ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْفُرْقَانَ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِ لِيَكُونَ لِلْعَالَمِينَ نَذِيرًا: "Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion upon His Servant that he may be to the worlds a warner."

And like verse 19 of Surah Al-An'am, which says: وَأُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنُ لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَمَنْ بَلَغَ: "And this Quran was revealed to me that I may warn you thereby and whomever it reaches."

In a hadith mentioned by some commentators in relation to the above verse, the universality of the Prophet's invitation is reflected as one of his great honors, where he says:

"I have been given five things—and I do not say this out of pride—I was sent to the red and the black (all races), the earth was made for me pure and a place of prostration (masjid), war booty was made lawful for me while it was not lawful for anyone before me, I have been aided by terror (cast into the hearts of enemies) such that it precedes me by a month's journey, and I have been given the station of Intercession (Shafa'ah), which I have reserved for my Ummah on the Day of Resurrection." [^1]

Although the above hadith does not explicitly interpret the verse, we have other narrations in this regard that either explicitly interpret the verse or use the expression "to all people" (للناس كافة), which is the same expression as the verse above [^2], and all indicate that the verse refers to the global nature of the Prophet's invitation.

Another interpretation mentioned for the verse is taken from the second meaning of "kaff," meaning "prevention." According to this interpretation, "kaffah" becomes an attribute of the Prophet [^3], and the meaning is: Allah sent the Prophet as a "preventer" for mankind from disbelief, disobedience, and sin. However, the first interpretation seems closer to the context.

In any case, since all humans possess the instinct to seek benefit and repel harm, the Prophets also held the stations of "bringer of glad tidings" (bashir) and "warner" (nadhir) to mobilize and activate both these instincts. However, the heedless and ignorant majority, without regard for their own destiny, rose up to oppose them and denied these great divine blessings.

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed; this hadith is also narrated from Ibn Abbas in al-Durr al-Manthur. [^2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, pp. 255 and 256. [^3]: Sometimes the "ta" (ة) is attached to the active participle (ism al-fa'il) to convey emphasis (mubalaghah) rather than femininity, like "rawiyah."

Since the previous verses alluded to the fact that Allah will gather all people on the Day of Resurrection and judge between them, the next verse quotes a question from the deniers of the Resurrection: "And they say, 'When is this promise, if you should be truthful?'" (وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَٰذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صَادِقِينَ).

The deniers of the Resurrection repeatedly asked this question of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله or other Prophets. Sometimes it was to understand the matter, but perhaps most often it was out of mockery—that if you are truthful, when will this Resurrection that you constantly emphasize finally arrive?

This implies that a truthful person must know all the details of what they report, being aware of its quality, quantity, time, and place.

However, the Quran always refrains from giving a direct answer to this and from determining the time of the Resurrection's occurrence, emphasizing that this is among the matters whose knowledge belongs exclusively to Allah, and no one besides Him is aware of it. Therefore, in the next verse, it expresses this same meaning with another phrasing, saying:

"Say, 'For you is the appointment of a Day [from which] you will not remain behind an hour, nor will you precede [it].'" (قُلْ لَكُمْ مِيعَادُ يَوْمٍ لَا تَسْتَأْخِرُونَ عَنْهُ سَاعَةً وَلَا تَسْتَقْدِمُونَ).

This concealment of the date of the Resurrection—even from the Prophet of Islam himself—as we have previously noted, is because Allah wants people to have a kind of freedom of action coupled with a state of constant readiness. For if the date of the Resurrection were determined, whenever its time was far off, everyone would sink into heedlessness, vanity, and ignorance; and whenever its time was near, they might lose their freedom of action and their deeds might take on a forced (compulsory) nature. In both cases, the educational goals for humanity would be frustrated. For this reason, the date of the Resurrection is hidden from everyone, just as the date of the Night of Power (Laylat al-Qadr)—the night that has the virtue of a thousand months—is hidden, or the date of the uprising of Imam al-Mahdi عليه السلام.

The expression found in verse 15 of Surah Taha: إِنَّ السَّاعَةَ آتِيَةٌ أَكَادُ أُخْفِيهَا لِتُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِمَا تَسْعَىٰ "Indeed, the Hour is coming—I almost conceal it—so that every soul may be recompensed for that which it strives," contains a subtle reference to this same meaning.

Furthermore, their assumption that if the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is telling the truth about the Resurrection, he must also know its exact date, is the height of error and evidence of their lack of awareness regarding the duty of Prophethood. He was only commissioned to convey the message, to bring glad tidings, and to warn. But the matter of the Resurrection belongs to Allah; He is the one aware of all its details and has provided His Prophet with only that portion deemed necessary for educational purposes.

Here a question arises: why does the Quran, in the position of threatening the opponents, say: "you will not remain behind the appointed time of the Resurrection for a moment" (la yasta'khirun), but also says "nor will you precede it"? What impact does this have on the Quran's objective?

In response, two points should be noted: First, mentioning these two together always indicates the certainty and precision of the timing of something, just as we say a certain matter "has no sooner or later," meaning its timing is definitive.

Second, a group of stubborn disbelievers constantly pressured the Prophets as to why this Resurrection does not come; in other words, they were in a hurry for it, whether out of mockery or otherwise. The Quran tells them: do not hurry; its date is exactly what Allah has ordained.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَن نُّؤْمِنَ بِهَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ وَلَا بِٱلَّذِى بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ ۗ وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ مَوْقُوفُونَ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ يَرْجِعُ بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ٱلْقَوْلَ يَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتُضْعِفُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أَنتُمْ لَكُنَّا مُؤْمِنِينَ (٣١)
The faithless say, ‘We will never believe in this Quran, nor in what was [revealed] before it.’ But if you were to see when the wrongdoers will be made to stop before their Lord casting the blame on one another. Those who were abased will say to those who were arrogant, ‘Had it not been for you, we would surely have been faithful.’ (31)
قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتُضْعِفُوٓا۟ أَنَحْنُ صَدَدْنَـٰكُمْ عَنِ ٱلْهُدَىٰ بَعْدَ إِذْ جَآءَكُم ۖ بَلْ كُنتُم مُّجْرِمِينَ (٣٢)
Those who were arrogant will say to those who were abased, ‘Did we keep you from guidance after it had come to you? No, you were guilty [yourselves].’ (32)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتُضْعِفُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ بَلْ مَكْرُ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ إِذْ تَأْمُرُونَنَآ أَن نَّكْفُرَ بِٱللَّهِ وَنَجْعَلَ لَهُۥٓ أَندَادًا ۚ وَأَسَرُّوا۟ ٱلنَّدَامَةَ لَمَّا رَأَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلْأَغْلَـٰلَ فِىٓ أَعْنَاقِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ هَلْ يُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٣٣)
Those who were abased will say to those who were arrogant, ‘No, [it was your] night and day plotting, when you prompted us to forswear Allah and to set up equals to Him.’ They will hide their remorse when they sight the punishment, and We will put iron collars around the necks of the faithless. Shall they not be requited for what they used to do? (33)

Commentary

In connection with the discussion in the preceding verses regarding the stance of the polytheists toward the issue of the Resurrection, the verses under discussion depict some of the painful scenes of the Hereafter for them, so they may become aware of the consequence of their conduct.

First, it says: "The disbelievers said: 'We will never believe in this Quran, nor in that which was before it'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ بِهذَا الْقُرْآنِ وَ لا بِالَّذِي بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ‌).

The word "لن" (lan), as we know, is used for perpetual negation. Therefore, they intend to say that even if you preach to us forever, we will never believe. This is evidence of their stubbornness, as they had made their decision for eternity. This is while a truth-seeking individual, if not convinced by one reason, cannot dismiss potential future reasons without hearing them and say, "I reject all other arguments in advance!" Regarding who is meant by "those who disbelieve" (الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا), a group of commentators has interpreted it as the polytheists, and some as the Jews and the People of the Book; however, the context of the subsequent verses, which speak of polytheism (shirk), indicates that the polytheists are intended.

The phrase "that which was before it" (بِالَّذِي بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ‌) refers to the Heavenly Books revealed to other prophets before the Quran, as this expression is used in many verses of the Quran with this meaning—especially after mentioning the Quran. The suggestion by some that it refers to "Resurrection" or "the content of the Quran" seems very unlikely.

In any case, the denial of faith in the books of previous prophets was perhaps because the Quran emphasizes that the signs of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) are clearly mentioned in the Torah and the Gospel. To negate the prophethood of the Prophet of Islam, they negate other Heavenly Books as well, saying: "We believe neither in this Book nor in the books before it!"

Then, addressing their state on the Day of Resurrection and speaking to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), it says: "If you could only see when these wrongdoers are made to stand before their Lord for reckoning and judgment (you would be amazed at their condition), while they cast the blame on one another, engaging in disputes and altercations" (وَ لَوْ تَرى‌ إِذِ الظَّالِمُونَ مَوْقُوفُونَ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ يَرْجِعُ بَعْضُهُمْ إِلى‌ بَعْضٍ الْقَوْلَ‌). [^1]

[^1]: "Yarji'u" is used both as an intransitive and a transitive verb; here it is the latter, conveying the meaning of "returning" or "throwing back" the speech. Since it is followed by "some of them to others" (بَعْضُهُمْ إِلى‌ بَعْضٍ‌), it results in the meaning of mutual exchange or disputation.

Once again, it is understood from the above verse that one of the most significant instances of "wrongdoing" (zulm) is "polytheism and disbelief."

The expression "before their Lord" (عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ) points to the fact that they are present before the One who was their Owner and Sustainer. What shame could be greater than for a person to be summoned before Someone in whom and in whose commands he never believed, while his entire existence was immersed in His blessings?

"In this state, the 'oppressed' (mustad'afin)—those ignorant individuals who blindly followed others—will say to the 'arrogant' (mustakbirin)—those who pursued the path of pride, vanity, and dominance over others, providing them with satanic ideological direction: 'Had it not been for you, had it not been for your deceptive and devilish whisperings, we would certainly have been among the believers'" (يَقُولُ الَّذِينَ اسْتُضْعِفُوا لِلَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا لَوْ لا أَنْتُمْ لَكُنَّا مُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

By this means, they wish to cast all their sins upon these heartless "arrogant ones," even though in the world they were not prepared to have such a decisive confrontation with them, because weakness and abjectness had overcome their being, and they had lost their freedom. But now that those false concepts that separated them from the arrogant have vanished, and the results of everyone's actions have become manifest, they stand face to face with them, speaking explicitly and aggressively.

But the arrogant will not remain silent; "in response to the oppressed, they will say: 'Did we bar you from guidance after it had come to you, and after the proof was sufficiently established and the prophets had said what needed to be said?'" (قالَ الَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا لِلَّذِينَ اسْتُضْعِفُوا أَ نَحْنُ صَدَدْناكُمْ عَنِ الْهُدى‌ بَعْدَ إِذْ جاءَكُمْ‌).

No, we are not responsible; "rather, you yourselves were guilty" (بَلْ كُنْتُمْ مُجْرِمِينَ‌), for despite having free will, you surrendered to our baseless words, turned toward disbelief and atheism, and consigned the logical words of the prophets to oblivion.

It is true that the arrogant committed a great sin with their whisperings, but their statement also holds a reality: these followers should not have followed them blindly, and in this respect, their sin lies upon themselves.

However, the "oppressed" are not satisfied with this answer, and they speak again to prove the guilt of the arrogant, "saying to the arrogant: 'Nay, but it was your plotting by night and day, and your deceitful propaganda that caused us to be barred from guidance, when you commanded us to disbelieve in Allah and to set up equals and peers for Him'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ اسْتُضْعِفُوا لِلَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا بَلْ مَكْرُ اللَّيْلِ وَ النَّهارِ إِذْ تَأْمُرُونَنا أَنْ نَكْفُرَ بِاللَّهِ وَ نَجْعَلَ لَهُ أَنْداداً).

Yes, it was you who did not cease your evil propaganda and did not miss any opportunity, night or day, to advance your sinister goals. It is true that we were free in our acceptance and are thus at fault and sinful, but you too are responsible and sinful as agents of corruption; rather, the first stone was laid by your impure hands, especially since you always spoke to us from a position of power (the expression "you commanded us" (تامروننا) is evidence of this).

Naturally, the arrogant can have no answer to this speech and cannot deny their partnership in this great crime.

Therefore, both groups regret their actions: the arrogant for misleading others, and the oppressed for the unconditional acceptance of these sinister whisperings. "But when they see the divine punishment, they will conceal their regret lest they be further disgraced, and We shall put shackles on the necks of the disbelievers" (وَ أَسَرُّوا النَّدامَةَ لَمَّا رَأَوُا الْعَذابَ وَ جَعَلْنَا الْأَغْلالَ فِي أَعْناقِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا).

Although concealing it in that world—which is the "Day of Manifestation" where nothing can be hidden—is of no use, they resort to secrecy out of an old habit they had in the world, under the illusion that they can hide their state.

Indeed, whenever they realized their mistake in the world and felt regret, they lacked the courage to express that regret, which is a prerequisite for returning and reconsidering; they employ this same moral characteristic in the Resurrection as well, but to what avail?

Some commentators have suggested that this concealing of regret is due to the intensity of the terror that strikes them upon witnessing the divine punishment and the placing of shackles on their necks; their breath is trapped in their chests and their tongues are silenced.

Nevertheless, those same individuals in other stages of the Resurrection will cry out: "Woe to us! Indeed, we were wrongdoers" (يا وَيْلَنا إِنَّا كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ‌) (Al-Anbiya, 14).

Some have also interpreted "asarrū" (أسرّوا) here to mean "manifested," stating that this word is used in the Arabic language for two opposite meanings, and such instances are not few. However, considering the usage of this word (israr) in the Quran and elsewhere, this meaning seems unlikely, as "sirr" (secret) is usually placed in opposition to "alan" (public). Al-Raghib in Al-Mufradat has also explicitly stated the weakness of this opinion, although some lexicographers have mentioned both meanings. [^1]

[^1]: In Lisan al-Arab, under the root "s-r-r," there is a detailed discussion on this matter, citing the differences among lexicographers and scholars of literature (Vol. 4, p. 357).

In any case, these are the results of their own deeds which they prepared beforehand: "Are they recompensed except for what they used to do?" (هَلْ يُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌).

Yes, it is these very acts and deeds of the disbelievers and criminals that are placed as chains of captivity around their necks, hands, and feet. In this world, they were also captives of their whims, wealth, power, and status; in the Resurrection, where the "embodiment of deeds" takes place, those same captivities appear in another form.

The above verse once again clarifies the issue of the "embodiment of deeds" (tajassum al-a'mal), which we have mentioned many times, for it speaks of the fact that "their recompense is their very deeds." What expression could be more vivid and clear than this for the embodiment of deeds? The expression "those who disbelieve" (الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا) is proof that both the arrogant deceivers and the oppressed who were deceived will meet this fate, and all disbelievers; fundamentally, the mention of this attribute indicates that the cause of their punishment is their very disbelief.

Ayahs

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا فِى قَرْيَةٍ مِّن نَّذِيرٍ إِلَّا قَالَ مُتْرَفُوهَآ إِنَّا بِمَآ أُرْسِلْتُم بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٣٤)
We did not send a warner to any town without its affluent ones saying, ‘We indeed disbelieve in what you have been sent with.’ (34)
وَقَالُوا۟ نَحْنُ أَكْثَرُ أَمْوَٰلًا وَأَوْلَـٰدًا وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ (٣٥)
They say, ‘We have greater wealth and more children, and we will not be punished!’ (35)
قُلْ إِنَّ رَبِّى يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَقْدِرُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٦)
Say, ‘Indeed my Lord expands the provision for whomever He wishes and He tightens it, but most people do not know.’ (36)
وَمَآ أَمْوَٰلُكُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُكُم بِٱلَّتِى تُقَرِّبُكُمْ عِندَنَا زُلْفَىٰٓ إِلَّا مَنْ ءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ جَزَآءُ ٱلضِّعْفِ بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَهُمْ فِى ٱلْغُرُفَـٰتِ ءَامِنُونَ (٣٧)
It is not your wealth, nor your children, that will bring you close to Us in nearness, excepting those who have faith and act righteously. It is they for whom there will be a twofold reward for what they did, and they will be secure in lofty abodes. (37)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَسْعَوْنَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا مُعَـٰجِزِينَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ مُحْضَرُونَ (٣٨)
As for those who contend with Our signs seeking to frustrate [their purpose], they will be brought to the punishment. (38)

Commentary

Wealth and Children Are Not Means of Nearness to Allah!

Since the previous verses spoke of the deception of the arrogant, the verses under discussion reflect a portion of this deception and simultaneously offer consolation to the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, telling him not to be surprised if they oppose him; for the opposition of the affluent arrogant against the true prophets has been their permanent method.

It says: "We never sent a warner to any city or land except that its mutrafun—those arrogant ones intoxicated by luxury and blessings—said: 'We are disbelievers in that with which you have been sent, and we do not accept what you call a Divine message'" (وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا فِي قَرْيَةٍ مِنْ نَذِيرٍ إِلَّا قَالَ مُتْرَفُوهَا إِنَّا بِمَا أُرْسِلْتُمْ بِهِ كَافِرُونَ).

"Nadhir" (warner) refers to the Divine prophets who warned the people of Divine punishment in response to deviations, injustices, sin, and corruption.

"Mutrafuha" is the plural of "mutraf," derived from the root "taraf" (rhyming with taraf), meaning luxury. A mutraf is someone whom the abundance of blessings and a comfortable life has made intoxicated, arrogant, and heedless, leading them to rebellion [^1]. Indeed, those who were usually in the front line of the opponents of the prophets were this group of rebellious and heedless mutrafun. This was because, on one hand, they saw the teachings of the prophets as an obstacle to their self-indulgence and whims; on the other hand, the prophets defended the rights of the deprived—those whose rights had been usurped to build these glamorous lives; and thirdly, they always relied on governmental power to guard their wealth, and they saw the prophets as being in direct opposition to them in all these aspects. Therefore, they immediately rose to combat them.

Surprisingly, they did not point to a specific ruling or teaching; rather, they said collectively: "We are disbelievers in all that you have been sent with," and we will not take even one step with you. This itself is the best evidence of their stubbornness and enmity against the truth.

This point is an important matter that the Quran has unveiled in various verses: that usually the deprived were the first to say "Labbayk" (respond) to the call of the prophets, and the arrogant affluent were the first group to raise the flag of opposition.

Although the deniers of the prophets' call were certainly not limited to this group, they were often the agents of corruption and the callers to polytheism and superstitions, constantly trying to force others into these paths as well.

The same meaning is found in Surah Az-Zukhruf verse 23, Surah Hud verse 116, and Surah Al-Mu'minun verse 33.

Not only against the prophets, but against every reformative step taken by any reformist scholar or mujahid scientist, this group rises in opposition. To break the programs of the reformers, they hatch conspiracies and do not shy away from any crime.

The next verse points to their hollow logic, which they resorted to in every era to prove their superiority and to deceive the common folk, saying: "And they said: 'We are more abundant in wealth and children'" (وَقَالُوا نَحْنُ أَكْثَرُ أَمْوَالًا وَأَوْلَادًا). They claimed that Allah loves them, as He has placed abundant wealth at their disposal as well as great human resources, and this is proof of His grace toward them and a sign of their status and position before Him, "and we (the favorites) will never be punished!" (وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ).

Does the Almighty punish His favorites?! If we were rejected from His presence, why would He give us all these blessings?! In short, the prosperity of our world is clear evidence of the prosperity of our Hereafter! Some commentators have suggested that the phrase "and we are not to be punished" is evidence that they denied the Resurrection and punishment altogether. However, the subsequent verses show that this phrase does not refer to that; rather, their meaning was that they are near to Allah because of their wealth.

The next verse responds to and crushes this hollow and deceptive logic in the most excellent way. Addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it says: "Say to them: 'My Lord expands the provision for whomsoever He wills, and restricts it (for whomsoever He wills)'" (and all of this is according to the wisdom He deems necessary for testing the creation and the system of human life, and has nothing to do with one's value and status in the sight of Allah) (قُلْ إِنَّ رَبِّي يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَنْ يَشَاءُ وَيَقْدِرُ).

Therefore, one should never consider the vastness of provision as a sign of felicity, nor the constriction of provision as a sign of wretchedness, "but most people do not know this reality" (وَلَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ).

Of course, the unaware and ignorant majority are like this; otherwise, this matter is clear and obvious to the knowledgeable.

Then, it pursues this same meaning with more clarity, saying: "It is never your wealth nor your children that bring you near to Us in station" (وَمَا أَمْوَالُكُمْ وَلَا أَوْلَادُكُمْ بِالَّتِي تُقَرِّبُكُمْ عِنْدَنَا زُلْفَى) [^1]. This is a great mistake that has afflicted a group of commoners who imagine that those who suffer from material deprivation in this world are the objects of Allah's wrath and rejection, and those who are immersed in luxury and blessings are His beloved and accepted ones. How many deprived individuals are tested through this means and reach the highest stations, and how many affluent individuals find their wealth and riches to be a calamity for their souls and a prelude to their punishment.

Does the Quran not explicitly say in Surah At-Taghabun, verse 15: "Your wealth and your children are only a trial, and with Allah is a great reward" (إِنَّمَا أَمْوَالُكُمْ وَأَوْلَادُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ وَاللَّهُ عِنْدَهُ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ).

This statement does not mean that man should abandon the necessary effort and striving for life; rather, the goal is that having economic means and abundant human resources never becomes the criterion for the spiritual value of humans in the presence of Allah.

Then, it turns to the primary criterion of human values and what causes nearness to Allah (in the form of a disconnected exception), saying: "Except those who believe and do righteous deeds; for them, there is a multiple reward for what they did, and they will reside in the high chambers (of Paradise) in ultimate security" (إِلَّا مَنْ آمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَالِحًا فَأُولَئِكَ لَهُمْ جَزَاءُ الضِّعْفِ بِمَا عَمِلُوا وَهُمْ فِي الْغُرُفَاتِ آمِنُونَ) [^1]. Therefore, all criteria return to these two things: "Faith" and "Righteous Action," from anyone, at any time, in any place, and from any class or group. The difference between humans in the presence of Allah is due to the difference in the degrees of their faith and the levels of their righteous deeds, and nothing else.

Even knowledge, science, and affiliation with great individuals—even with prophets—if not accompanied by these two criteria, do not by themselves add anything to a person's value.

Here, the Quran, with its unparalleled clarity, draws a line of cancellation through all deviant and superstitious thoughts regarding the factors of nearness to the Lord and the existential value of man. It summarizes the authentic criterion in two things that all humans have the power to acquire, and in which material possibilities and deprivations are not influential.

Indeed, wealth and children, if placed in this path, take on this same Divine hue and accept the color of faith and righteous action, becoming a means of nearness to Allah. But wealth and children that distance man from Allah, and are worshipped like an idol, becoming a source of corruption and misguidance, are the kindling of Hell. As the Quran says: they are the enemies of man's soul and his happiness:

"O you who believe! Indeed, among your spouses and your children are enemies to you, so beware of them!" (At-Taghabun: 14).

Additionally, as we have previously mentioned, "di'f" (double/multiple) does not only mean "twofold," but also means "several-fold," and in the verse under discussion, it carries this meaning.
