For we know that the reward for every good deed with Allah is at least ten times (An-An'am: 160), and sometimes it goes far beyond that.

"Ghurufat" is the plural of "ghurfah," meaning rooms located on the upper floor which have more light, better air, and are far from pests. For this reason, this term is used for the highest dwellings of Paradise.

This word originally comes from the root "gharf" (rhyming with barf), meaning to lift or pick something up.

The expression "aminun" (those who are in security) regarding the people of Paradise is a very comprehensive term that reflects their spiritual and physical tranquility from every aspect. For they have no fear of annihilation, decline, or death; nor fear of an enemy's attack; nor of illness, pestilence, or sorrow; nor even the fear of fear! And there is no blessing greater than for a person to live in security from every aspect, just as there is no calamity worse than insecurity in various aspects of life.

In the next verse, it describes the opposite group, saying: "But those who strive against Our signs to frustrate them—neither having faith themselves nor allowing others to step on the path of truth, while imagining they can escape the grasp of Our power—they will be brought into the painful punishment of the Day of Resurrection" (وَالَّذِينَ يَسْعَوْنَ فِي آيَاتِنَا مُعَاجِزِينَ أُولَئِكَ فِي الْعَذَابِ مُحْضَرُونَ).

They are the same ones who, using their wealth, children, and numbers, engaged in denying the prophets and busy themselves with whispering to Allah's creation. They were so arrogant that they thought they could escape the grasp of Divine punishment, but all of them, by Allah's command, will be brought into the heart of the blazing fire of Hell.

The phrase "they are brought into the punishment" (أُولَئِكَ فِي الْعَذَابِ مُحْضَرُونَ), since there is no mention of future time in it, might refer to the fact that they are already entangled in punishment. What punishment is greater than this prison they have built for themselves with wealth and children?! It is also possible that this expression is used because this Divine promise is so certain that it is as if they are in it now, just as it was said: "they are in the high chambers in security."

The term "mu'ajizin," as some linguists have stated, means that they imagine they can escape from the sphere of Allah's power and His punishment, while this is a vain and baseless thought [^1].

Key Points Evaluating Values!

The important issue in the life of an individual and societies is the issue of the criteria of measurement and the value system governing the culture of that society.

This is because all movements in the life of the individual and the collective stem from this very value system and are for the creation of these values.

The mistake of a people or nation in this matter, and turning toward imaginary and baseless values, is enough to lead their history to ruin. Understanding real values and true criteria is the firmest foundation for the palace of their happiness.

Arrogant world-worshippers consider value to be limited only to wealth, material powers, and their numbers. They even imagine the criterion of personality in the presence of Allah within this framework, as we saw an example of in the above verses, and many other examples of it are found in the Holy Quran.

  1. Pharaoh, the tyrant and worshipper of gold and power, says to his associates: "I do not believe Moses is from Allah; if he is telling the truth, then why have gold bracelets not been given to him?!" (Surah Az-Zukhruf, verse 53). He even considered the lack of such gold and jewelry as evidence of the lowliness of Moses' station and said: "Am I not better than this one who is despicable?" (Surah Az-Zukhruf, verse 52).

  2. The polytheists of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله era were surprised that the Quran was revealed to a poor man, saying: "Why was this Quran not revealed to a great man (a wealthy man) from the two cities (Mecca or Ta'if)?" (Az-Zukhruf, verse 31).

  3. The Children of Israel objected to "Ishmu'il" (Samuel), the prophet of their time, regarding the selection of "Talut" (Saul) as the commander of the army, saying: "We are more deserving of kingship than him, and he has not been given an abundance of wealth!" (Surah Al-Baqarah, 247).

  4. The wealthy polytheists of Noah's people objected to him: "Why have these low and despicable people surrounded you?!" And by "despicable," they meant lacking wealth and riches! "They said: 'Shall we believe in you while the lowest of the low follow you?'" (Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verse 111).

  5. The wealthy of Mecca made the same objection to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: "Why have the barefooted ones surrounded you? We are even bothered by the smell of their bodies; if you drive them away, we will be by your side." The Quran in Surah Al-Kahf attacks them severely, threatens them with the harshest tone, and commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "You must sit with men who, though poor, have hearts full of love for Allah and turn to His presence morning and evening, seeking nothing but Him. O Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, stay with these and never turn your eyes away from them" (Al-Kahf: 28).

Based on these points, the first and most important reformative step of the prophets was to break these false value frameworks. Through their teachings, they dismantled these wrong criteria and replaced them with authentic Divine values. Through a "cultural revolution," they changed the axis of personality from wealth, children, riches, status, and the fame of tribe and family to piety, faith, and righteous action.

We read an example of this in the verses under discussion, where after drawing a line of cancellation through "wealth" and "children" as a means of nearness in the Divine presence and saying "It is never your wealth nor your children that bring you near to Us in station," it immediately replaces it with the authentic value through the phrase "Except those who believe and do righteous deeds."

The noble verse: "Indeed, the most noble of you in the sight of Allah is the most pious of you," which has become an Islamic slogan, expresses this same intellectual and value revolution after negating values dependent on tribe and clan.

According to this verse (Surah Al-Hujurat: 13), nothing except piety—faith accompanied by a sense of responsibility and purity of action—is the criterion for evaluating human personality and their nearness to Allah. Whoever has a greater share of this authentic criterion is nearer and more noble.

Interestingly, in the environment of Arabia before the life-giving teachings of Islam and the Quran appeared, due to the dominance of the value system of gold and power, the product of that environment was a handful of plunderers and bullies like the "Abu Sufyans," "Abu Jahls," and "Abu Lahabs."

But from that same environment, after the revolution of the value system, the "Salmans," "Abu Dharrs," "Miqdads," and "Ammar Yasirs" arose.

It is noteworthy that the Holy Quran in Surah Az-Zukhruf, after mentioning the verses referred to above, says: "Not only is material glamour not evidence of personality, but if it were not for the fact that corruption would arise from this path, We would have provided for the disbelievers houses with roofs of silver and (expensive) stairs and ladders by which they could ascend to the upper floors, and for their rooms, (glamorous) doors and (beautiful) couches upon which to recline, and We would have provided them with all kinds of ornaments. But all these are the enjoyments of the life of this world, and the home of the Hereafter with your Lord is for the pious" (Az-Zukhruf, verses 33-35).

All of this is so that false values do not take the place of real human values.

Ayahs

قُلْ إِنَّ رَبِّى يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ وَيَقْدِرُ لَهُۥ ۚ وَمَآ أَنفَقْتُم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَهُوَ يُخْلِفُهُۥ ۖ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ (٣٩)
Say, ‘Indeed my Lord expands the provision for whomever of His servants that He wishes and tightens it, and He will repay whatever you may spend, and He is the best of providers.’ (39)
وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ يَقُولُ لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ أَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ إِيَّاكُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْبُدُونَ (٤٠)
On the day He will muster them all together, He will say to the angels, ‘Was it you that these used to worship?’ (40)
قَالُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ أَنتَ وَلِيُّنَا مِن دُونِهِم ۖ بَلْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْبُدُونَ ٱلْجِنَّ ۖ أَكْثَرُهُم بِهِم مُّؤْمِنُونَ (٤١)
They will say, ‘Immaculate are You! You are our wali, not they! No, they used to worship the jinn; most of them had faith in them.’ (41)
فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا يَمْلِكُ بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ نَّفْعًا وَلَا ضَرًّا وَنَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ ٱلَّتِى كُنتُم بِهَا تُكَذِّبُونَ (٤٢)
‘Today you have no power to benefit or harm one another,’ and We shall say to those who did wrong, ‘Taste the punishment of the Fire which you used to deny.’ (42)

Commentary

The Disavowal of the Worshipped from the Worshippers

Once again, these verses return to answering the claims of those who imagined their wealth and children were proof of their proximity to Allah's threshold. As an emphasis, it says: "Say: My Lord expands the provision for whomsoever He wills of His servants or restricts it" (قُلْ إِنَّ رَبِّي يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ وَ يَقْدِرُ لَهُ‌).

Then it adds: "And whatever thing you spend [in the way of Allah], He replaces it; and He is the best of providers" (وَ ما أَنْفَقْتُمْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ فَهُوَ يُخْلِفُهُ وَ هُوَ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ‌).

Although the content of this verse emphasizes the previous point, it contains novelty in two aspects:

First, the previous verse, which carried the same concept, was mostly directed at the wealth and children of the disbelievers, whereas the use of the term "servants" (عِباد) in the verse under discussion shows it is directed at the believers. That is, even regarding the believers, He sometimes expands provision—where it is in the believer's best interest—and sometimes tightens and restricts it—where His wisdom necessitates it. In any case, the abundance or scarcity of livelihood cannot be proof of anything.

Second, the previous verse described the expansion and restriction of livelihood regarding two different groups, while the verse under discussion may refer to two different states of a single person, whose provision is sometimes expanded and sometimes tightened and restricted.

Furthermore, what appears at the beginning of this verse is, in fact, a prelude to what is at the end of the verse, which is the encouragement to spend in the way of Allah.

The phrase "فَهُوَ يُخْلِفُهُ‌" (He replaces it) is a beautiful expression showing that whatever is spent in the way of Allah is, in reality, a highly profitable trade, because Allah has taken the responsibility of its replacement upon Himself. We know that when a generous person takes responsibility for replacing something, he does not observe mere equality and parity, but rather multiplies it several times, and sometimes a hundredfold.

Of course, this divine promise is not limited to the Hereafter and the next world—though that is certain in its place—but in this world too, He replaces the spent wealth in the best manner with various types of blessings.

The phrase "هُوَ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ‌" (He is the best of providers) has a broad meaning and can be examined from various dimensions:

He is better than all providers because He knows what to grant and how much provision to give so that it does not become a cause of corruption and ruin, for He is All-Knowing of everything.

He can grant whatever He wills, for He is All-Powerful over everything.

He seeks no reward or recompense for what He grants, for He is Self-Sufficient by His very Essence.

He gives even without being asked, for He is aware of everything and is All-Wise.

In fact, no one is truly a "provider" except Him, for whatever anyone possesses is from Him, and anyone who gives something to another is merely a "medium of transfer of provision," not the provider.

It is also noteworthy that He gives "everlasting" blessings in exchange for "perishing" wealth, and grants "much" in exchange for "little."

Since this group of oppressive and rebellious wealthy people were among the polytheists and claimed, "We worship the angels and they are our intercessors on the Day of Resurrection," the Quran proceeds to answer this baseless claim as well, saying: "And [mention] the Day He will gather them all—both the worshippers and the worshipped—then He will say to the angels: 'Did these people used to worship you?'" (وَ يَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ يَقُولُ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ أَ هؤُلاءِ إِيَّاكُمْ كانُوا يَعْبُدُونَ‌).

Clearly, this question is not one intended to uncover an unknown for the Pure Essence of Allah, for He is All-Knowing of everything. The goal is for the truths to be spoken by the angels so that this group of worshippers becomes disgraced and ashamed, and realizes that the angels are completely disassociated from their actions, leading them to despair forever.

The mention of "angels" from among all the deities the polytheists had is either because the angels were the most noble creatures they worshipped—if no intercession comes from them on the Day of Resurrection, what could possibly come from a handful of stones and wood, or from jinn and devils?! Or it is from the perspective that the idolaters considered stones and wood as manifestations and symbols of celestial beings (angels and the souls of prophets) and worshipped them under this title.

As mentioned in the history of idol worship among the Arabs, "Amr ibn Luhay" [^1] on a journey to Sham (Levant) saw a group worshipping idols. He asked them about this, and they said: "These are gods we have made in the form of celestial beings; we seek help from them and request rain through them!" Amr ibn Luhay liked this practice and followed them, bringing an idol back with him to the Hijaz as a gift. From that time, idol worship began and spread until Islam came and uprooted it. [^2]

Now let us see what the "angels" say in response to the Lord's question. They choose the most comprehensive and polite answer, submitting: "Exalted are You, O Lord, from these false attributions they have made to Your Holy Presence" (قالُوا سُبْحانَكَ‌).

We have had no connection with this group whatsoever; "You are our Guardian (Wali) rather than them" (أَنْتَ وَلِيُّنا مِنْ دُونِهِمْ‌).

"They did not worship us; rather, they used to worship the jinn, and most of them were believers in them" (بَلْ كانُوا يَعْبُدُونَ الْجِنَّ أَكْثَرُهُمْ بِهِمْ مُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

There is discussion among commentators regarding the meaning of the angels' response, and each has provided an interpretation. However, what seems closer [to the truth] is that "jinn" refers to Satan and other wicked beings who encouraged the idolaters toward this act and beautified it in their eyes. Therefore, the worship of the jinn means obedience and following their commands and accepting their whisperings.

While expressing hatred and disavowal and declaring their lack of consent for this act, the angels say that the primary cause of this corruption was the devils, even though they outwardly worshipped us; the true face of this act must be exposed.

In this way, they drive away the worshippers in every sense and make them lose hope.

We had a similar meaning in Surah Yunus, where it says: "And [mention] the Day We will gather them all together; then We will say to those who associated others [with Allah], 'Remain in your place, you and your partners.' Then We will separate them, and their partners will say, 'You did not used to worship us!'" (Yunus: 28).

That is, you were actually worshipping your own whims, desires, illusions, and imaginations, not us. Furthermore, this worship of yours was neither by our command nor with our consent, and worship that is such is not truly worship.

In this way, the hope of the polytheists on that day turns into complete despair, and this truth becomes clearly evident to them: that their deities will not untie the smallest knot of their problems; rather, they are disgusted by them and disavow them. Therefore, in the next verse, as a meaningful conclusion, it says: "So today none of you possesses for another [any] benefit or harm" (فَالْيَوْمَ لا يَمْلِكُ بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ نَفْعاً وَ لا ضَرًّا).

Thus, neither the angels—who were ostensibly their deities—can intercede, nor can they help one another.

"It is here that We say to those who wronged: 'Taste the punishment of the Fire which you used to deny'" (وَ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا ذُوقُوا عَذابَ النَّارِ الَّتِي كُنْتُمْ بِها تُكَذِّبُونَ‌).

This is not the first time the Quran refers to the polytheists as "wrongdoers" (ظالم). In many other verses of the Quran, "disbelief" (kufr) is referred to as "wrongdoing" (zulm), or "disbelievers and polytheists" are referred to as "wrongdoers." This is because, before anything else, they wrong themselves by removing the proud crown of servitude to the Lord from their heads and placing the humiliating collar of servitude to idols around their necks, wasting all their dignity, personality, and destiny.

In reality, on the Day of Resurrection, they see both the punishment for their polytheism and the punishment for denying the Resurrection, and both meanings are combined in the phrase: "And We say to those who wronged: 'Taste the punishment of the Fire which you used to deny.'"

Key Points

1- Spending Leads to Increase, Not Decrease

The expression we read in the above verses regarding spending—where it says that whatever you spend in the way of Allah, He replaces it—is a very meaningful expression.

Firstly: From the perspective that "thing" (شى‌ء) in the broad sense of the word includes all types of spending, whether material or spiritual, small or large, to any needy person, whether young or old. The important thing is that a person gives from their existing capital in the way of Allah, in whatever quality or quantity it may be.

Secondly: it takes spending out of the concept of "annihilation" and gives it the color of "permanence," because Allah has guaranteed to fill its place with His material and spiritual blessings, which are several times—sometimes thousands of times, and at least ten times—greater. Thus, when a person enters the field with this spirit and belief, they will be more open-handed and generous. They never allow the feeling of deficiency or the thought of poverty into their mind; rather, they thank Allah for granting them success in such a profitable trade.

This is the same expression that the Holy Quran has used in verses 10 and 11 of Surah As-Saff: "O you who have believed, shall I lead you to a trade that will save you from a painful punishment? [It is that] you believe in Allah and His Messenger and strive in the cause of Allah with your wealth and your lives. That is best for you, if you should know."

In a narration from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said:

"Every night a caller from heaven cries out: 'Give birth for death!' And another caller cries out: 'Build for ruin!' And a caller cries out: 'O Allah! Grant a replacement for the one who spends.' And another caller cries out: 'O Allah! Grant destruction for the one who withholds!' And another caller says: 'If only people had not been created!' And another caller shouts: 'If only now that they are created, they would reflect on why they were created!'" [^1] (The meaning of these callers who cry out are the angels who manage the affairs of this world by Allah's command).

In another Hadith from him, we read:

"Whoever is certain of the replacement will be generous in spending!" [^2] The same meaning has been narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام.

However, the important issue is that the spending must be from lawful (halal) and legitimate wealth, for Allah does not accept anything else and does not grant it blessing.

Thus, in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: A person said to him, "There are two verses in the Quran that no matter how much I seek them, I do not find them (i.e., I do not achieve their content)."

The Imam asked, "Which verses?"

He replied, "First is this word of Allah the Great who says: 'Call upon Me; I will respond to you.' I call upon Allah, but my prayer is not answered!" He said: "Do you think Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has broken His promise?"

He said, "No."

He asked, "Then what is the reason for it?"

He said, "I do not know."

He said, "But I will inform you: Whoever obeys Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, in what He commanded regarding prayer, and observes the proper direction of prayer, He will answer him."

He asked, "What is the direction of prayer?" He said: "First you praise Allah, and remember His blessings, then you give thanks, then you send blessings upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, then you remember your sins and confess to them and seek refuge in Allah from them, and you repent; this is the direction of prayer!" Then he said: "What is the other verse?"

He replied: "It is this verse that says: 'And whatever thing you spend, He replaces it; and He is the best of providers.' But I spend in the way of Allah, yet nothing that fills its place comes back to me!" The Imam عليه السلام said: "Do you think Allah has broken His promise?!" He said: "No."

He asked: "Then why is it so?"

He said: "I do not know."

He said: "If one of you were to acquire wealth from its lawful source and spend it in its lawful way, he would not spend a single Dirham except that Allah would replace it for him." [^1]

2- Insure Your Wealth with Divine Insurance:

One of the commentators has an interesting analysis here, saying:

It is strange that a merchant, when he knows one of his goods is at risk of perishing, is willing to sell it even on credit, even if the party is poor. He says: "This is better than letting it stay and be destroyed."

And if a merchant in such conditions does not take action to sell his goods until they are destroyed, they consider him "erroneous"! And if in such conditions a wealthy buyer is found and he does not sell to him, they describe him as "senseless."

And if, in addition to all this, the buyer, despite having financial capability, offers all kinds of collateral and writes a reliable document, yet he still does not sell to him, they call him "insane"!

But the wonder is that we all do these things and no one considers it insanity! For all our wealth is certainly subject to vanishing and will inevitably leave our hands, and spending in the way of Allah is a kind of loan to Him, and a very reliable guarantor—namely Allah the Great—has said: "And whatever thing you spend, He replaces it." At the same time, He has left His properties as collateral with us, for whatever is in man's hand is a loan from Him (and the strongest documents from the Heavenly Books have been placed at our disposal in this regard).

Yet despite all this, many of us do not spend our wealth and let it leave our hands; we have neither reward nor gratitude. [^1]

3- The Breadth of the Concept of "Spending" (Infaq)

To know how broad the concept of "spending" is in Islam, it is enough to consider the following Hadith:

The Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"Every good deed, in whatever form it may be, is considered charity (Sadaqah) and spending in the way of Allah (and it is not limited to financial spending).

And whatever a man spends for the needs of his own life and his family is written as charity.

And that with which a man protects his honor is considered charity.

And whatever a man spends in the way of Allah, its replacement will be given to him, except for what was spent on a building (like building a house!) or spent in the way of sin." [^2] The exception of the house may be from the perspective that its essence remains, and furthermore, people's greatest attention is toward it.

[^1]: Amr ibn Luhay was one of the prominent figures of Mecca. [^2]: Tafsir "Ruh al-Ma'ani," Vol. 22, p. 140 (under the verse discussed). In the "Sira" of Ibn Hisham, the same meaning appears with a slight difference, and there we read that he brought the idol "Hubal" from Sham with him (Sira Ibn Hisham, Vol. 1, p. 79).

Ayahs

وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ قَالُوا۟ مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا رَجُلٌ يُرِيدُ أَن يَصُدَّكُمْ عَمَّا كَانَ يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُكُمْ وَقَالُوا۟ مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ إِفْكٌ مُّفْتَرًى ۚ وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٤٣)
When Our manifest signs are recited to them, they say, ‘This is just a man who desires to keep you from what your fathers used to worship.’ And they say, ‘This is nothing but a fabricated lie.’ The faithless say of the truth when it comes to them: ‘This is nothing but plain magic,’ (43)
وَمَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُم مِّن كُتُبٍ يَدْرُسُونَهَا ۖ وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْهِمْ قَبْلَكَ مِن نَّذِيرٍ (٤٤)
though We did not give them any scriptures that they might have studied, nor did We send them any warner before you. (44)
وَكَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ وَمَا بَلَغُوا۟ مِعْشَارَ مَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ فَكَذَّبُوا۟ رُسُلِى ۖ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ نَكِيرِ (٤٥)
Those who were before them had also denied—and these have not attained one-tenth of what We had given them—they impugned My apostles, so how was My rebuttal! (45)

Commentary

With What Logic Do They Deny Our Verses?

The preceding verses spoke of the condition of the polytheists and faithless individuals on the Day of Resurrection. The verses under discussion once again address their state in this world, recounting their reaction upon hearing the Quran, so it becomes clear that their grim fate in the Hereafter is the result of this erroneous stance against Divine verses in this world.

First, it says: "And when Our clear verses are recited to them, they say, 'This is only a man who wishes to avert you from that which your fathers used to worship'" (وَ إِذا تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا بَيِّناتٍ قالُوا ما هذا إِلَّا رَجُلٌ يُرِيدُ أَنْ يَصُدَّكُمْ عَمَّا كانَ يَعْبُدُ آباؤُكُمْ‌).

This is their first reaction to these "clear verses" (آيات بينات), which they raised in order to provoke the sense of prejudice (asabiyyah) in this fanatical people.

Specifically, the expression "your fathers" (آبائكم) instead of "our fathers" (آبائنا) is intended to make that fanatical tribe feel that the heritage of their ancestors is in danger: "Arise and cut off this man's hand from it!" The expression "this is only a man..." (ما هذا إِلَّا رَجُلٌ‌) was intended to belittle the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله from two perspectives: first, the word "this" (هذا), and second, "a man" (رجل) in an indefinite form, even though they all knew the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله well and were aware of his clear history.

It is also noteworthy that the Quran describes the "verses" as "clear" (بينات), meaning the proofs of their truthfulness are contained within them; that which is self-evident requires no further explanation.

Then, it mentions the second argument they raised to invalidate the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله call, stating: "And they say, 'This (Quran) is nothing but a great lie forged against Allah!'" (وَ قالُوا ما هذا إِلَّا إِفْكٌ مُفْتَرىً‌).

"Ifk" (إفك), as previously mentioned, refers to anything that is turned away from its original form; hence, contrary winds are called "mu'tafikat." Subsequently, the word "ifk" was applied to lies, slander, and any false speech, though some say "ifk" refers specifically to "great lies."

Although the term "ifk" was sufficient to accuse the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله of lying, they emphasized it with the word "muftara" (forged), without having any evidence for this claim of theirs.

Finally, the third accusation they leveled against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was the charge of "sorcery," as we read at the end of the verse: "And those who disbelieve say of the truth when it has come to them, 'This is nothing but manifest sorcery!'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلْحَقِ‌ لما جاءهم‌إِنْ هذا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُبِينٌ‌).

It is remarkable that this misguided group expressed all three of their accusations with the most explicit emphasis—the form of restriction (hasr). In one place they said: "This is only sorcery"; in another: "This is only a lie"; and finally: "He only wants to turn you away from the deities of your ancestors."

Of course, these three false attributions are not contradictory—though they did not shy away from contradictory speech—therefore, there is no reason to attribute each of these accusations to a different group of disbelievers, as some commentators have suggested.

It is also noteworthy that in the first and second instances, the Quran uses the phrase "they said" (قالوا), but in the third stage, it uses "those who disbelieve said" (قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا). This points to the fact that all these misfortunes stem from disbelief, the denial of truth, and enmity toward reality; otherwise, how could a person, without any evidence, shower all these accusations one after another upon a man whose proofs of truthfulness are clear from his speech, his actions, and his past?

It seems that with these three accusations, they were following a calculated plan in their struggle against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. On one hand, they saw that it was a new religion with great attraction.

On the other hand, the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله warnings of Divine punishment in this world and the Hereafter inevitably terrified a group of people.

Thirdly, the miracles of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله inevitably had an effect on the souls of the masses.

To neutralize each of these three issues, they had devised a scheme: against this new religion, they raised the issue of preserving the heritage of the ancestors, even though their ancestors, according to the Quran, were those who "did not understand anything nor were they guided" (لا يَعْقِلُونَ شَيْئاً وَ لا يَهْتَدُونَ) (Al-Baqarah, 170).

There is no sin in averting people from such superstitious customs which are the heritage of ignorant fools.

Against the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله warnings of Divine punishment, they raised the issue of lying to pacify the masses.

And against the miracles, they raised the accusation of "sorcery" to explain them away and prevent people from being drawn to them.

But as we know, and as the history of Islam testifies, none of these satanic whisperings were effective, and eventually, people entered this pure religion in crowds.

In the next verse, the Quran strikes a line of nullification through all their claims, even though their falsehood is already clear. It answers all their vain claims with a single sentence, saying: "And We had not given them any scriptures which they could study, nor did We send to them before you any warner" (وَ ما آتَيْناهُمْ مِنْ كُتُبٍ يَدْرُسُونَها وَ ما أَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْهِمْ قَبْلَكَ مِنْ نَذِيرٍ).

This indicates that such claims can only be raised by someone to whom a prophet has previously come and brought a heavenly book, and who finds the content of the new call to be in conflict with it and thus rises to deny it. Sometimes such a person says, "Do not let the religion of your ancestors be lost," sometimes he says, "This new call is a lie," and sometimes he calls its bringer a sorcerer.

But someone who—relying solely on his own thought, without any heavenly revelation, and possessing no share of knowledge—has woven together superstitions, has no right to make such a judgment.

From this verse, it is also inferred that man cannot traverse the ups and downs of life's path solely by the power of his own intellect; rather, he must seek help from the power of revelation and walk with the guidance of the "Khidr of Prophethood," otherwise there is darkness, and he must fear the danger of going astray.

In the final verse under discussion, He threatens this rebellious group with an effective and eloquent statement, saying: "And those before them denied [the Divine verses]" (وَ كَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ‌).

"While these [people] have not reached even a tenth of what We gave to those [former peoples]" (وَ ما بَلَغُوا مِعْشارَ ما آتَيْناهُمْ‌).

But see what their fate became? Yes, "They denied My messengers; then how [terrible] was My reproach!" (فَكَذَّبُوا رُسُلِي فَكَيْفَ كانَ نَكِيرِ).

Their ruined cities, under the crushing blows of Divine punishment, are near you and on your path toward Sham (Levant). Take them as a mirror of warning, hear the necessary lessons from the tongue of these ruins, and compare your fate to theirs; for neither is the Divine Sunnah (way) changeable, nor are you superior to them! "Mish'ar" (معشار) is derived from the root "ushr" (عشر) and carries the same meaning (one-tenth).

Some have interpreted it as "ushr-ushr," meaning one-hundredth, but most lexicons and commentaries mention the first meaning. In any case, such numbers are not for literal counting but are used for diminution, in contrast to numbers like seven, seventy, and a thousand, which are used for multiplication (abundance).

Therefore, the concept of the verse is that We crushed powerful tyrants, while these people do not possess even a small fraction of their power! Similar meanings appear in several other verses of the Quran, including Surah Al-An'am, verse 6: "Have they not seen how many generations We destroyed before them, whom We had established in the land as We have not established you? And We sent [rain from] the sky upon them in abundance and made rivers flow beneath them; then We destroyed them for their sins and brought forth after them a generation of others."

Similar meanings are also found in Surah Al-Mu'min, verse 21, and Surah Ar-Rum, verse 9.

The word "nakir" (نكير) is from the root "inkar" (انكار) and carries the same meaning; what is meant by Allah's "inkar" is His punishment and torment. [^1]

[^1]: Some commentators have suggested another possibility in interpreting this verse: that the phrase "And they have not reached a tenth of what We gave them" means that even one-tenth of the verses provided to the polytheists of the Quraysh to complete the proof (itmam al-hujjah) were not available to previous nations. Since We punished the predecessors with such severity, the state of the Quraysh polytheists—for whom the proof has been completed ten times over—is clear. However, the first interpretation seems more appropriate. (According to the first interpretation, of the four pronouns in the verse, the first and second refer to the Quraysh disbelievers, and the third and fourth refer to the previous polytheists. But according to the second interpretation, the first pronoun refers to the Quraysh polytheists, the second to the previous disbelievers, the third to the Quraysh polytheists, and the fourth to the previous disbelievers—observe closely).

Ayah

۞ قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَعِظُكُم بِوَٰحِدَةٍ ۖ أَن تَقُومُوا۟ لِلَّهِ مَثْنَىٰ وَفُرَٰدَىٰ ثُمَّ تَتَفَكَّرُوا۟ ۚ مَا بِصَاحِبِكُم مِّن جِنَّةٍ ۚ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ لَّكُم بَيْنَ يَدَىْ عَذَابٍ شَدِيدٍ (٤٦)
Say, ‘I give you just a single advice: that you rise up for Allah’s sake, in pairs or singly, and then reflect: there is no madness in your companion; he is just a warner to you before [the befalling of] a severe punishment.’ (46)

Commentary

Intellectual Revolution, the Root of Every Authentic Revolution

In this section of verses and the upcoming ones, which form the concluding discussions of this Surah, the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله is once again commanded to invite the people toward the Truth using various arguments and to deter them from deviation. As in previous discussions, the Prophet is addressed five times with the command: "Say to them..." (قُلْ...).

In the first verse, the essence of all social, moral, political, economic, and cultural transformations is highlighted. In very brief yet profound sentences, it says: "Say to them: I only advise you of one thing: that you stand up for Allah, in pairs or individually, and then reflect" (قُلْ إِنَّما أَعِظُكُمْ بِواحِدَةٍ أَنْ تَقُومُوا لِلَّهِ مَثْنى‌ وَ فُرادى‌ ثُمَّ تَتَفَكَّرُوا).

"Your companion (Muhammad) has no madness or intellectual deviation" (ما بِصاحِبِكُمْ مِنْ جِنَّةٍ).

Rather, "He is only a warner to you before a severe punishment from Allah" (إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ لَكُمْ بَيْنَ يَدَيْ عَذابٍ شَدِيدٍ).

Each word and expression in this verse points to an important matter, ten points of which we present below:

  1. The phrase "I advise you" (أَعِظُكُمْ) actually expresses the reality that "I consider only your benefit and well-being in this statement, and nothing else."

  2. The expression "one thing" (واحِدَةٍ), especially emphasized by "only" (إِنَّما), is an eloquent indication of the fact that the root of all individual and collective reforms is the activation of thought. As long as a nation's intellect is asleep, they will be raided by the thieves of religion, faith, freedom, and independence; but when thoughts awaken, the path is blocked for the plunderers.

  3. The term "stand up" (قِيام) here does not mean standing on two feet, but rather readiness to perform a task. When a person stands on their feet, they become ready to carry out various life programs. Therefore, reflection requires prior readiness—an internal motivation and movement that leads a person to engage in thinking with will and determination.

  4. The expression "for Allah" (لِلَّهِ) indicates that this standing and readiness must have a Divine motive. Reflection originating from such a motive is valuable. Fundamentally, sincerity in actions and even in thinking is the essence of salvation, happiness, and blessing. Interestingly, belief in "Allah" is taken as a given here; thus, the reflection is for other matters, suggesting that Monotheism (Tawhid) is an innate (fitri) matter that is clear even without deep reflection.

  5. The expression "in pairs or individually" (مَثْنى‌ وَ فُرادى‌) indicates that reflection and thought should be far from clamor and commotion. People should stand up individually or, at most, in pairs, and employ their intellect. This is because reflection amidst noise and uproar will not be deep, especially since factors of ego and prejudice in defending one's beliefs are more likely to arise in a crowd. Some commentators have also suggested that these two expressions are intended to encompass both "individual" and "collective" (consultative) thinking. A person should think alone and also benefit from the thoughts of others, as autocracy in thought and opinion leads to ruin. Collective thinking and efforts to solve scientific problems together—provided it does not lead to clamor—certainly have a better effect, which is perhaps why "pairs" is mentioned before "individually."

  6. Interestingly, the Quran says here "reflect" (تَتَفَكَّرُوا), but on what? In this regard, it is absolute, and as the rule goes, "the omission of the object indicates generality." This means reflecting on everything: spiritual life, material life, major issues, minor issues—in short, one must first think before every action. Most importantly, however, is reflection to find answers to these four questions: Where have I come from? Why have I come? Where am I going? And where am I now? However, some commentators believe that the object of "reflection" here is the subsequent sentence: (ما بِصاحِبِكُمْ مِنْ جِنَّةٍ), meaning: "If you reflect a little, you will clearly realize that the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله is pure and free from your baseless accusation of madness." The first meaning seems clearer. Nevertheless, among the matters that must be reflected upon is this very issue of Prophethood and the outstanding qualities, intellect, and wisdom present in the person of the Prophet of Islam, without being limited to it.

  7. The expression "your companion" (صاحِبِكُمْ) regarding the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله points to the fact that he is not an unknown figure to you. He has been among you for many years; you have known him for his trustworthiness, wisdom, and truthfulness. Until now, you have observed no weakness in his life record. Therefore, be fair and realize that the accusations you level against him are all baseless.

  8. "Jinnah" (جِنَّةٍ) meaning madness, originally comes from the root "jinn" (covering/veiling). Since a madman is such that his intellect seems covered, this term is used for him. In any case, the noteworthy point here is that it seems to express this truth: how can one who invites others to reflection and the awakening of thought be mad himself? The very fact that he is the herald of reflection is proof of his ultimate intellect and wisdom.

  9. The sentence "He is only a warner to you" (إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ لَكُمْ‌) summarizes the Prophet's mission in the matter of "warning" (indhar)—that is, warning about responsibilities, the Divine court, and punishment. It is true that the Prophet also has the mission of giving glad tidings (basharat), but what primarily drives a person to movement is the issue of warning. Hence, in some other verses of the Quran, it is mentioned as the Prophet's sole duty, such as in Surah Al-Ahqaf, verse 9: "I am but a clear warner" (وَ ما أَنَا إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ). Similar meanings appear in Surah Sad, verse 65, and other verses.

  10. The expression "before a severe punishment" (بَيْنَ يَدَيْ عَذابٍ شَدِيدٍ) indicates that the Resurrection is so near that it is as if it is right in front of you. Indeed, compared to the lifespan of the world, it is so. This expression also appears in Islamic narrations where the Holy Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله said: "My being sent and the coming of the Hour are like these two"—then he صلي الله عليه وآله joined his middle and index fingers together and showed them. [1]

Key Points

1. The Foundation of All Transformations

Materialistic and communist schools, which always feel threatened by true religions, constantly insist on portraying the call of religions as an opiate for the masses. Their infamous expression "Religion is the opium of the masses" is well-known. Similarly, the colonialists of the East and West, out of fear of the uprising of the faithful masses under the shadow of religious thought and their welcoming of martyrdom for Allah, tried to indoctrinate their psychologists and sociologists to reflect in their so-called scientific books that religion is the product of human ignorance regarding natural factors! Of course, this is an extensive discussion, and decisive, crushing answers have been given to them in their proper place, which cannot be fully detailed here. However, verses such as the ones under discussion, which invite reflection and thought—and indeed consider this very reflection to be the essence of religion and the leaven for human perfection and progress—expose these liars.

[1]: Tafsir "Ruh al-Ma'ani," under the verse discussed, Vol. 22, p. 143.

How can a faith like Islam be a means of sedation or a product of ignorance when its bringer, with his loudest voice, addresses all humans and says: "Rise up and revolt to revive dormant thoughts, and do so in a calm environment free from clamor"? In an environment far from whims, desires, and poisonous propaganda waves. Far from prejudices and far from obstinacy. Stand up for Allah and reflect. For this is my only advice to you and nothing else! Is it not ridiculous to accuse such a faith—which repeats this invitation not only here but in numerous instances—of sedating thoughts?! Especially since it says not only to reflect in solitude but also in pairs and with mutual assistance; listen to the content of the prophets' call, study their arguments, and if they harmonize with your intellect, accept them.

The events that occurred in our era due to the uprising of revolutionary Muslims in various countries against the hellish powers of the East and West—which darkened the world in the eyes of the arrogant and shook the foundations of their power—showed that they had correctly understood that authentic religious beliefs are their fierce enemy and a great danger to them. It also showed the true purpose behind the accusations they leveled against religion. Truly, it is strange that in the so-called philosophical analyses of Western sociologists, they take it as a given that there is no world beyond nature and that religion is a man-made phenomenon. Then they argue over what its cause is: Is it economic issues? Is it human fear? Is it a lack of human awareness? Is it psychological complexes, and so on? But they are not willing, even for a moment, to empty themselves of this false prejudice and consider the possibility that there is another world beyond the natural world, and to reflect on the clear proofs of Monotheism and the manifest signs of the prophethood of messengers like Muhammad صلي الله عليه وآله. These people are not unlike the polytheists of the Age of Ignorance, with the difference that those were prejudiced, obstinate, and uneducated, while these are prejudiced, obstinate, but educated! For this reason, they are more dangerous and deceptive! Interestingly, the concluding part of many Quranic verses is an invitation to reflection (tafakkur), reasoning (ta'aqqul), or remembrance (tadhakkur): Sometimes it says: "Indeed in that is a sign for a people who reflect" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ) (An-Nahl: 11 and 69). And sometimes: "Indeed in that are signs for a people who reflect" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ) (Ar-Ra'd: 3, Az-Zumar: 42, and Al-Jathiyah: 13). And sometimes: "That they may reflect" (لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ) (Al-Hashr: 21 and Al-A'raf: 176). And sometimes it presents this phrase directly, saying: "Thus Allah makes clear to you the signs that you may reflect" (كَذلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ لَكُمُ الآيات لعلكم تتفكرون) (Al-Baqarah: 219 and 266). There are many such instances in the Quran, such as the invitation to "fiqh" (understanding) in many verses, the invitation to "aql and ta'aqqul" (intellect and reasoning) and the praise of those who employ their intellect, and the severe condemnation of those who do not use their thought, which appears in 46 verses of the Holy Quran! The extensive descriptions of scholars, the status of knowledge, and science—if we were to gather and interpret all such verses—would form an independent book.

In this regard, it suffices that the Quran mentions the lack of reflection and reasoning as one of the characteristics of the people of Hell: "And they will say: 'If only we had listened or reasoned, we would not be among the companions of the Blaze!'" (For the place of the rational is not in Hell) (Al-Mulk: 10). And elsewhere it says: Fundamentally, individuals who have ears but do not hear, eyes but do not see, and intellect but do not reflect, are candidates for Hell! "And We have certainly created for Hell many of the jinn and mankind. They have hearts with which they do not understand, they have eyes with which they do not see, and they have ears with which they do not hear. Those are like cattle; rather, they are more astray. It is they who are the heedless" (Al-A'raf: 179).

2. A Glimpse of Islamic Narrations Regarding Thought and Reflection

In Islamic narrations—following the Quran—the issue of thought and reflection is placed in the highest degree of importance, and very eloquent and interesting expressions are seen regarding it, examples of which we bring here:

A. Reflection is the Greatest Worship

In a hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, we read: "Worship is not [merely] a large amount of prayer and fasting; rather, worship is reflection upon the affair of Allah, the Almighty and Majestic" (i.e., the secrets of the world of creation). [1] In another narration, we read: "The majority of Abu Dharr's worship was reflection and thinking." [2]

B. One Hour of Reflection is Better than a Night of Worship

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that someone asked about what people narrate from the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله: "An hour of reflection is better than a night of standing [in prayer]." What does it mean, and how should one reflect? The Imam عليه السلام replied: "One passes by a ruin or a house [empty of its inhabitants] and says: 'Where are your inhabitants? Where are your builders? Why do you not speak?'" [3]

C. Reflection is the Source of Action

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says: "Indeed, reflection invites toward goodness and acting upon it." [4]

[1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Book of "al-Kufr wa al-Iman," Chapter on Reflection (p. 45). [2]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 383, under the root "Fikr." [3] & [4]: Ibid.

Ayahs

قُلْ مَا سَأَلْتُكُم مِّنْ أَجْرٍ فَهُوَ لَكُمْ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ (٤٧)
Say, ‘Whatever reward I may have asked you is for your own good. My [true] reward lies only with Allah, and He is witness to all things.’ (47)
قُلْ إِنَّ رَبِّى يَقْذِفُ بِٱلْحَقِّ عَلَّـٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ (٤٨)
Say, ‘Indeed my Lord hurls the truth. [He is] the knower of all that is Unseen.’ (48)
قُلْ جَآءَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَمَا يُبْدِئُ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ وَمَا يُعِيدُ (٤٩)
Say, ‘The truth has come, and falsehood neither originates nor restores [anything].’ (49)
قُلْ إِن ضَلَلْتُ فَإِنَّمَآ أَضِلُّ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِى ۖ وَإِنِ ٱهْتَدَيْتُ فَبِمَا يُوحِىٓ إِلَىَّ رَبِّىٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ سَمِيعٌ قَرِيبٌ (٥٠)
Say, ‘If I go astray, my going astray is only to my own harm, and if I am rightly guided that is because of what my Lord has revealed to me. Indeed He is all-hearing and nearmost.’ (50)

Commentary

Falsehood Can Accomplish Nothing!

We have stated that in this series of verses, Allah commands His Prophet five times to speak to these faithless, misguided people through various means and to close every path of excuse for them from every direction.

In the previous verse, the discussion was about the call to reflection and the negation of any mental imbalance in the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

In the first verse under discussion, the topic is the lack of demanding any wage or reward in exchange for the mission of Risalah (prophethood).

It says: "Say: Whatever reward I might have asked of you is for yourselves" (قُلْ ما سَأَلْتُكُمْ مِنْ أَجْرٍ فَهُوَ لَكُمْ).

"My reward is only upon Allah" (إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلَى اللَّهِ).

This is an indication that a rational person must have a motive for everything he does. Once the perfection of my intellect is proven to you, and you see that I have no material motive, you must realize that a divine and spiritual incentive has driven me to this task.

In other words: I invited you to reflect; now think and ask your conscience: what has caused me to warn you of a severe divine punishment? What profit comes to me from this work? And what material benefit does it hold for me?

Furthermore, if your excuse for this aversion and turning away from the truth is that you must pay a heavy price for it, I have fundamentally asked no reward or wage from you.

As this same meaning is explicitly stated in verse 46 of Surah Al-Qalam: أَمْ تَسْئَلُهُمْ أَجْراً فَهُمْ مِنْ مَغْرَمٍ مُثْقَلُونَ "Or do you ask them a reward, so that they are burdened by a debt?"

Regarding what the phrase "it is for yourselves" (فَهُوَ لَكُمْ) signifies, there are two interpretations:

First, it may be an allusion to the absolute lack of demanding any wage, similar to when we say: "Whatever I asked of you is yours," meaning I asked for nothing. The evidence for this is the subsequent phrase: إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلَى اللَّهِ "My reward is only upon Allah."

Second, if you see that in some of my words brought from the Lord I have said to you: لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى "I do not ask you for this any reward except love for [my] kinsfolk" (Ash-Shura: 23).

This too, its benefit returns to you, because love for the kinsfolk (Dhul-Qurba) relates back to the issue of "Imamate and Wilayah" and the "continuation of the line of Prophethood," which is also necessary for the continuation of your guidance.

The evidence for this is a context of revelation (Shan al-Nuzul) that some have narrated here: when the verse قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى was revealed, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to the polytheists of Mecca: "Do not distress my kinsfolk." They accepted this proposal. However, when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله spoke against their idols, they said: "Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله does not treat us fairly; on one hand, he asks us not to harm his kinsfolk, but on the other hand, he harms us by speaking ill of our gods." At this point, the verse: قُلْ ما سَأَلْتُكُمْ مِنْ أَجْرٍ فَهُوَ لَكُمْ (the verse under discussion) was revealed and told them: "What I asked of you in this regard was for your own benefit; now, whether you wish to harm them or not [is up to you]." 1

And at the end of the verse, He says: "And He is Witness over all things" (وَ هُوَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ شَهِيدٌ).

If I seek my reward from Him, it is because He is aware of all my actions and intentions.

1: Tafsir "Ruh al-Bayan," Vol. 7, p. 308.

Furthermore, He is the witness to my truthfulness, for He has placed all these miracles and clear signs at my disposal.

And truly, He is the greatest witness, for He who knows the realities better than anyone, and can express them better than anyone, and from whom nothing but truth proceeds, is the best of witnesses, and He is Allah.

With regard to what was said concerning the truthfulness of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله call, the next verse states: The Quran is an undeniable reality cast from Allah upon the heart of the Prophet. "Say: Indeed, my Lord hurls the truth; [He is] the Knower of the unseen" (قُلْ إِنَّ رَبِّي يَقْذِفُ بِالْحَقِّ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوبِ).

Considering that "yaqdhifu" comes from the root qadhf, meaning to throw to a distant point or to hurl from afar, several interpretations have been given for this verse, which are compatible with one another:

First: It refers to the hurling of "Truth"—meaning the Heavenly Books and Divine Revelation—upon the hearts of the Prophets and messengers of the Lord. Because He, by virtue of being the "Knower of the unseen," recognizes and chooses prepared hearts and hurls the revelation into them so that it penetrates their depths.

In this way, it is not unlike the famous Hadith: "Knowledge is a light that Allah hurls into the heart of whomever He wills"—those He deems worthy.

The expression "Knower of the unseen" (Allam al-Ghuyub) supports this meaning.

Others have said it refers to hurling the truth against falsehood and crushing falsehood by means of the truth. That is, the truth possesses such power that it removes all obstacles from its path, and no one has the power to oppose it. Thus, it is a threat to the opponents not to rise against the Quran, and to know that the truth of the Quran will crush them.

In this case, it is similar to what appears in verse 18 of Surah Al-Anbiya: بَلْ نَقْذِفُ بِالْحَقِّ عَلَى الْباطِلِ فَيَدْمَغُهُ فَإِذا هُوَ زاهِقٌ "Rather, We hurl the truth against falsehood, and it knocks it out, and behold, it is vanished."

The possibility has also been raised that the expression "hurling" (qadhf) here refers to the penetration of the Quran's truth into the far and near corners of the world, indicating that eventually, this heavenly revelation will encompass the world and illuminate everywhere with its light.

Then, for further emphasis, He adds: "Say: The truth has come, and falsehood can neither originate [anything] nor repeat [it]" (قُلْ جاءَ الْحَقُّ وَ ما يُبْدِئُ الْباطِلُ وَ ما يُعِيدُ). 1 Thus, falsehood will have no role against the truth—neither a new, initiating role nor a repetitive one—because its plans are brought to naught, and for this very reason, it cannot cover the light of truth or erase its effect from memories.

Although some commentators have tried to restrict "truth" and "falsehood" in this verse to limited instances, it is evident that both concepts are broad and expansive. The Quran, divine revelation, and Islamic teachings are all gathered under the concept of "Truth."

And "shirk" (polytheism), disbelief, misguidance, oppression, sin, satanic whisperings, and tyrannical innovations are all included in the meaning of "Falsehood."

In reality, this verse is similar to verse 81 of Surah Al-Isra, which says: وَ قُلْ جاءَ الْحَقُّ وَ زَهَقَ الْباطِلُ إِنَّ الْباطِلَ كانَ زَهُوقاً "And say: Truth has come, and falsehood has departed. Indeed, falsehood is [by nature] ever bound to depart."

In a narration from Ibn Mas'ud, it is stated that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله entered Mecca while there were 360 idols around the House of Allah. With a stick he held in his hand, he knocked down the idols one by one, saying:

جاءَ الْحَقُّ وَ زَهَقَ الْباطِلُ إِنَّ الْباطِلَ كانَ زَهُوقاً جاء الحق و ما يبدئ الباطل و ما يعيد

1

Question

A question arises here: the above verse says that with the appearance of truth, "falsehood" fades away and completely loses initiative, yet we see that falsehood still gallops about and has brought many regions under its dominance?

In response, one must note several points: First, with the appearance and manifestation of truth, falsehood—meaning polytheism, disbelief, hypocrisy, and what stems from them—loses its color. If it continues its life, it is through force, oppression, and pressure; otherwise, its mask has been removed, and its hideous face has been revealed to truth-seekers. This is the meaning of the coming of truth and the vanishing of falsehood.

Second: For the realization of the government of truth and the decline of the government of falsehood across the world, in addition to the means Allah has placed at the disposal of His servants, the existence of certain conditions on their part is also necessary. The most important of these is the arrangement of preliminaries to utilize these means.

In other words: the victory of truth over falsehood is based on two foundations: the "agency of the actor" (fa'iliyyat al-fa'il) and the "receptivity of the recipient" (qabiliyyat al-qabil), not only in ideological, logical, and teleological aspects but also in executive aspects. If it does not reach victory in the executive stage due to the non-realization of receptivity, it is not evidence of its lack of victory [in principle].

Just as the Quran says, for example: ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ "Call upon Me; I will respond to you" (Ghafir: 60). But we know that the answering of prayer is not unconditional; if its conditions are met, its response is certain, otherwise, one should not expect a response. The explanation of this meaning is found under verse 186 of Surah Al-Baqarah (Vol. 1, p. 639).

This is exactly like bringing a skilled physician to the bedside of a patient. We say, "The means for your salvation are provided," and when we prepare the medicine for him, we say, "Your problem is solved." However, all of these are "conducive factors" (muqtadi), not the "complete cause" (illat-e tammah). The patient must use the medicine, follow the physician's conditions, and not forget the necessary precautions so that the healing finds external reality (reflect on this).

  • Then, to clarify that what he says is from Allah, and every guidance is from Him, and that no error ever occurs in divine revelation, He adds:

"Say: If I should err, I would only err against myself, and if I am guided, it is by what my Lord reveals to me" (قُلْ إِنْ ضَلَلْتُ فَإِنَّما أَضِلُّ عَلى‌ نَفْسِي وَ إِنِ اهْتَدَيْتُ فَبِما يُوحِي إِلَيَّ رَبِّي). 1

1: Regarding why "ala" (against) is used in the first phrase (ala nafsi) and "bi" (by/with) in the second (fabima yuhi...), some commentators have said each of these two phrases has an omitted part that is understood from the other, and the implied meaning was: "If I err, I only mislead myself, and if I am guided, I am only guided for myself by what my Lord reveals to me" (reflect on this) (Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, under the verse).

That is to say: I too, if left to myself, would go astray, because finding the path of truth amidst the mass of falsehoods is impossible except with the help of the Lord, and the light of guidance in which no misguidance can enter is the light of His revelation.

It is true that the intellect is a brilliant lamp, but we know that man is not infallible, and the beam of this lamp cannot pierce all the veils of darkness. So you too, come and grasp the mantle of this light of divine revelation so that you may emerge from the valley of darkness and step into the land of light.

In any case, where the Prophet, with all his knowledge and awareness, would not reach anywhere without divine guidance, the situation of others is clear.

And at the end of the verse, He adds: "Indeed, He is Hearing and Near" (إِنَّهُ سَمِيعٌ قَرِيبٌ).

Lest you think He does not hear our words and yours, or that He hears but is far from us; no, He is both Hearing and Near. Therefore, not an atom of our conversations and desires is hidden from Him.

Ayahs

وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذْ فَزِعُوا۟ فَلَا فَوْتَ وَأُخِذُوا۟ مِن مَّكَانٍ قَرِيبٍ (٥١)
Were you to see them when they will be stricken with terror, without any escape, and are seized from a close quarter. (51)
وَقَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِهِۦ وَأَنَّىٰ لَهُمُ ٱلتَّنَاوُشُ مِن مَّكَانٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (٥٢)
They will say, ‘We believe in it [now]!’ But how can they attain it from a far-off place, (52)
وَقَدْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَيَقْذِفُونَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ مِن مَّكَانٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (٥٣)
when they denied it in the past—shooting at something invisible from a far-off place— (53)
وَحِيلَ بَيْنَهُمْ وَبَيْنَ مَا يَشْتَهُونَ كَمَا فُعِلَ بِأَشْيَاعِهِم مِّن قَبْلُ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ فِى شَكٍّ مُّرِيبٍۭ (٥٤)
and a barrier is set between them and what they desire, as was done aforetime with their likes, who had remained in grave doubt? (54)

Commentary

They Have No Way of Escape

In the verses under discussion, which are the final verses of Surah Saba, and in light of the previous discussions regarding the stubborn polytheists, the address is once again directed toward the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). It vividly portrays the state of this group when they are caught in the grip of divine punishment—how they think of believing only after being ensnared, yet their faith will be of no avail.

He says: "If you could but see when they are terrified but there is no escape, and they cannot flee from the grip of divine punishment, and they are seized from a place nearby, you would be amazed at their helplessness" (وَ لَوْ تَرى‌ إِذْ فَزِعُوا فَلا فَوْتَ وَ أُخِذُوا مِنْ مَكانٍ قَرِيبٍ) [^1]. There is a discussion among commentators as to when this cry, terror, and restlessness occur:

Some consider it related to the punishment in this world or at the moment of death, while others relate it to the punishment of the Day of Resurrection.

However, in the final verse under discussion, there is an expression indicating that these verses all pertain to this world and the "punishment of extirpation" (adhab al-istisal), or the moment of death. This is because the last verse says: "And a barrier is set between them and what they desired, just as was done with their kind before."

This expression is inconsistent with the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, for on that day, everyone is gathered in one place for the reckoning, as stated in verse 103 of Surah Hud: "That is a Day for which mankind will be gathered together, and that is a Day witnessed [by all]" (ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَجْمُوعٌ لَهُ النَّاسُ وَ ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَشْهُودٌ). And in verse 50 of Surah Al-Waqi'ah, we read: "Say: Indeed, the former and the latter [generations] will surely be gathered together for the appointment of a known Day" (قُلْ إِنَّ الْأَوَّلِينَ وَ الْآخِرِينَ لَمَجْمُوعُونَ إِلى‌ مِيقاتِ يَوْمٍ مَعْلُومٍ).

[^1]: "وَ لَوْ تَرى‌" is a conditional sentence whose result (jaza) is omitted; it is implied as: "...you would have seen a momentous matter" or "you would have been amazed at their state."

Therefore, the meaning of the phrase "أُخِذُوا مِنْ مَكانٍ قَرِيبٍ" (seized from a place nearby) is that these faithless and oppressive individuals not only cannot escape the sphere of God's power, but Allah seizes them from a place that is very close to them! Were not the people of Pharaoh buried in the waves of the Nile, which was the source of their pride? Did not Qarun sink into the earth amidst his treasures? And were not the people of Saba—whose story appeared in this very Surah—seized from the nearest place, namely that great dam which was the heart of their prosperity and the source of their life and movement? Thus, Allah seizes them from the nearest place so they may recognize His manifestation of power.

Many tyrannical kings were killed and destroyed by those closest to them, and many oppressive powerful figures received their final blow from within their own homes.

And if we see that in numerous narrations transmitted through both Shi'a and Sunni chains, this verse is applied to the uprising of "Sufyani" (a group following the school of Abu Sufyan, the remnants of the Age of Ignorance who rise against the supporters of truth on the eve of the uprising of the Mahdi) and his army—who, while moving toward Mecca with the intent to conquer it, are trapped in the desert, and a severe earthquake causes the earth to split and swallow them—it is, in fact, an explanation of one of the instances of "أُخِذُوا مِنْ مَكانٍ قَرِيبٍ," as they are caught in the grip of divine punishment from the very spot beneath their feet.

The content of this Hadith has been narrated from the Noble Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) by Ibn Abbas, Ibn Mas'ud, Abu Hurairah, Abu Hudhayfah, Umm Salama, and Aisha, as recorded in Sunni books [^1]. Many Shi'a commentators, such as those of Tafsir al-Qummi, Majma' al-Bayan, Nur al-Thaqalayn, and Al-Safi, as well as a group of Sunni commentators like the authors of Ruh al-Ma'ani, Ruh al-Bayan, and Al-Qurtubi, have also cited it under the verses in question.

The late Allamah Majlisi narrated several reports in Bihar al-Anwar from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) and the Noble Prophet of Islam in this regard, showing that one of the applications of the verses under discussion is the issue of the Sufyani's uprising at the time of the Mahdi's (عليه السلام) advent, where Allah seizes and destroys them from the nearest place [^2]. As we have stated many times, narrations appearing in the interpretation of verses often mention clear instances and are never a reason for limiting the general concept of the verses.

In the next verse, He describes their state when caught in the grip of divine punishment, saying: "They say: 'We believe in it' (the Quran, its bringer, the Origin, and the Return)" (وَ قالُوا آمَنَّا بِهِ) [^3] "But how can they reach it from a place so far?!" (وَ أَنَّى لَهُمُ التَّناوُشُ مِنْ مَكانٍ بَعِيدٍ). Yes, with the arrival of death and the punishment of extirpation, the doors of return are completely closed, and a firm barrier is seemingly created between man and the compensation for past wrongdoings. For this reason, the declaration of faith at that moment is as if it is being made from a distant point.

Fundamentally, such faith—which is of a desperate nature and due to the extraordinary terror of the punishment they witness with their own eyes—has no value. Hence, we read in other verses of the Quran: "They are liars; even if they were sent back, they would surely return to what they were forbidden" (Al-An'am: 28).

"Tanawush" is derived from the root "nawsh" (rhyming with khawf), meaning to take hold of something. Some have defined it as taking something with ease; meaning, how can they easily reach such a distant goal?

How can they, at this moment when everything has ended, seek to compensate for their errors and believe, "while they had disbelieved in it before"—in that state where they possessed full choice and freedom of will—(وَ قَدْ كَفَرُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ)? Not only did they disbelieve, but they also leveled various accusations against the Prophet of Islam and his teachings, and "they would make conjectures about the Unseen—the metaphysical world, the Resurrection, and the Prophet's mission—making incorrect judgments and hurling [accusations] at it from a far-off place" (وَ يَقْذِفُونَ بِالْغَيْبِ مِنْ مَكانٍ بَعِيدٍ).

"Qadhf," as we have said, means to throw something, "Ghayb" is the world beyond the senses, and "makan ba'id" means a distant point. Collectively, it is a subtle metaphor for someone who judges the metaphysical world without knowledge or information. Just as throwing something from a distant point rarely hits the target, their conjecture, suspicion, and judgment also fail to hit the mark.

Sometimes they called the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) a "sorcerer," sometimes "insane," sometimes a "liar," and sometimes they considered the Quran a product of human thought. At other times, they completely denied Paradise, Hell, and the Resurrection. All of this was a kind of "stoning the unseen," "a shot in the dark," and "hurling from a far-off place."

Then He adds: "Finally, a barrier is set between them and what they desired by means of death, just as was done with their kind before" (وَ حِيلَ بَيْنَهُمْ وَ بَيْنَ ما يَشْتَهُونَ كَما فُعِلَ بِأَشْياعِهِمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ). In one painful moment, they see all their wealth and riches, all their palaces and positions, and all their desires being separated from them. Those who clung tightly to every dirham and dinar and would not let go of the slightest material possibility—what will be their state at that moment when they must bid farewell to it all at once, close their eyes, and step toward a dark and terrifying future?

For the phrase "حِيلَ بَيْنَهُمْ وَ بَيْنَ ما يَشْتَهُونَ" (a barrier is set between them and what they desired), two interpretations have been provided:

First is the interpretation mentioned above. The second is that they wish to believe and compensate for the past, but a barrier will be placed between them and this request.

However, the first interpretation is more consistent with the meaning of the phrase "what they desire" (ma yashtahun). Furthermore, in the preceding verses, the phrase (أَنَّى لَهُمُ التَّناوُشُ مِنْ مَكانٍ بَعِيدٍ) already addressed the issue of their inability to access faith at the time of death and the punishment of extirpation, so there is no need for repetition.

It also seems necessary to mention that many commentators have considered these verses as referring to the punishment of the Day of Resurrection and the regret of sinners in the arena of the Gathering. However, as we said, the final verse under discussion, considering the phrase "كَما فُعِلَ بِأَشْياعِهِمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ" (as was done with their kind before), is not consistent with this meaning; rather, it refers to the moment of death and the witnessing of Allah's destructive punishment.

And how beautifully Commander of the Faithful Ali (عليه السلام) speaks, depicting the moments of death and separation from the blessings of the world in his luminous words in the clearest way: "The stupor of death and the regret of losing [worldly blessings] come upon them; their limbs become feeble and their color changes! Then death increases its entry into them, until a barrier is placed between one of them and his speech, while he is among his family, looking with his eyes and hearing with his ears..."

"He thinks about what he exhausted his life in? And in what he spent his time? He remembers the wealth he collected, closing his eyes to its sources, taking it from both the clear and the doubtful!..."

"He bites his hand in regret over what became clear to him at the time of death regarding his affairs, and he becomes indifferent to what he used to desire during the days of his life. He wishes that the one who used to envy him for it and was jealous of him had acquired it instead of him!" [^1]

Finally, in the last sentence of the verse under discussion, He says: "The cause of all these issues is that they were constantly in a state of suspicious doubt," and naturally, such a fate awaited them (إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا فِي شَكٍّ مُرِيبٍ).

O Lord! Make us among those who wake up before opportunities pass and strive to compensate for what has been lost. O Allah! The snare of the world is difficult, and the enemy is powerful and strong; if Your grace does not assist us, our state is wretched.

O God! Make us among those who give thanks when blessings arrive and do not become arrogant or heedless, and who do not despair when calamities strike but rather take a lesson.

End of Surah Saba March 1, 1984 (17 Jumada al-Ula 1404 AH)
